proposals.title: Leveraging Single-Cell DNA Sequencing and Patient-Derived Models to Quantify the Impact of Intra-Tumor Heterogeneity on PI3K/AKT Inhibitor Response Prediction in ER+ Metastatic Breast Cancer
proposals.motivation: Pre-clinical drug testing relies heavily on clonal cell lines, which fail to capture the intra-tumor heterogeneity (ITH) present in patient tumors. This is a critical limitation in ER+ metastatic breast cancer, where resistance to targeted agents like PI3K/AKT inhibitors (e.g., capivasertib) often emerges from minor, pre-existing subclones. Understanding how ITH shapes therapeutic outcomes is essential for improving predictive models and combination strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the diversity and clonal architecture of PTEN/PIK3CA mutations within a tumor, measurable by single-cell genomics, determine the depth and durability of response to PI3K/AKT inhibitor combinations (e.g., capivasertib + fulvestrant) more accurately than bulk sequencing or clonal cell line models.
proposals.method: We will perform single-cell DNA sequencing (using the 10x Genomics CNV or similar platform) on pre-treatment biopsies from a cohort of PTEN-mutant ER+ MBC patients treated with capivasertib + fulvestrant. Parallel bulk NGS and generation of patient-derived organoids (PDOs) from the same samples will be conducted. Using computational deconvolution algorithms (e.g., CopyKAT, inferCNV) and phylogenetic reconstruction tools (e.g., SCITE), we will map subclonal mutation patterns and correlate them with clinical response and the emergence of resistance, as tracked by serial cfDNA analysis. The predictive power of these ITH metrics will be compared to that of traditional bulk sequencing and PDO drug screening results.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework for integrating ITH into pre-clinical drug response models. It will directly test the clinical relevance of subclonal PTEN/PIK3CA alterations and identify ITH features that predict early resistance to PI3K/AKT inhibition. The findings could guide more rational patient stratification and the design of combination therapies targeting heterogeneous tumor populations.
proposals.novelty: While ITH is widely acknowledged, this study uniquely applies single-cell DNA sequencing directly to a clinically annotated cohort from a targeted therapy trial, enabling a direct, quantitative link between subclonal architecture and a specific, mechanistically defined treatment outcome, moving beyond correlative associations.

proposals.title: In Vivo Validation of Cell Line-Derived PI3K/AKT Inhibitor Sensitivity Biomarkers in PTEN-Altered ER+ Breast Cancer
proposals.motivation: Pre-clinical cell line models have identified complex network biomarkers and resistance mechanisms for PI3Kα/AKT inhibitors in ER+ breast cancer, but their clinical relevance remains uncertain. Validating these biomarkers in more physiologically relevant models is critical to translate them into predictive tools for trials combining agents like capivasertib with fulvestrant, especially in PTEN-mutant populations where response is heterogeneous.
proposals.hypothesis: Biomarkers of drug sensitivity and resistance identified in ER+ breast cancer cell line network models, particularly those related to PI3K/AKT and ER signaling crosstalk, will predict therapeutic response in patient-derived xenograft (PDX) models and correlate with clinical outcomes in metastatic breast cancer cohorts treated with PI3K/AKT inhibitors.
proposals.method: We will test top biomarker candidates from prior cell line network analyses (e.g., specific phospho-protein nodes, gene expression signatures) in a panel of ER+ PDX models with known PTEN status. PDXs will be treated with fulvestrant ± capivasertib/alpelisib, and response will be correlated with biomarker status via RNA-seq and RPPA. Biomarkers validated in PDXs will then be assessed in serial cfDNA and tumor biopsies from a phase I basket trial cohort using targeted NGS and digital PCR, analyzing for association with progression-free survival.
proposals.contribution: This work will bridge a key gap between computational cell line discoveries and clinical application by providing rigorous in vivo validation. It will deliver a refined, PDX-tested biomarker panel for patient stratification in PI3K/AKT inhibitor trials and offer insights into the mechanistic fidelity of cell line-derived network models for predicting in vivo therapeutic vulnerabilities.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have identified biomarkers in cell lines or clinical samples separately, this proposal systematically tests cell line-derived network model predictions in a sequential PDX-to-patient pipeline, explicitly focusing on the context of PTEN alteration and combination endocrine therapy, which is not fully explored.

proposals.title: Genomically-Guided Combinatorial Drug Screening to Overcome Resistance in ER+ Metastatic Breast Cancer
proposals.motivation: Despite the efficacy of endocrine therapy combined with PI3K/AKT pathway inhibitors (e.g., fulvestrant + capivasertib) in PTEN-mutant ER+ metastatic breast cancer, intrinsic and acquired resistance remains a major clinical challenge. Pre-2026 work, including Baselga's own studies, has identified candidate resistance biomarkers from single-agent screens and network models, but these have not been systematically leveraged to design rational, biomarker-stratified drug combinations to preempt or overcome resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: That drug combinations selected based on genomic stratification—specifically by mutations (e.g., PTEN, ESR1, PIK3CA) and predicted resistance pathways from network models—will yield superior and more durable anti-tumor responses in patient-derived models of ER+ breast cancer compared to standard-of-care combinations.
proposals.method: We will perform high-throughput combinatorial drug screening on a panel of well-characterized ER+ breast cancer cell lines and patient-derived organoids (PDOs), stratified by PTEN, PIK3CA, and ESR1 mutation status. Combinations will be prioritized using pre-2026 algorithms: 1) SynergyFinder (BLISS or Loewe models) to quantify interaction scores from dose-response matrices, and 2) logic-based network models of ER+ signaling, trained on prior single-agent data, to predict synergistic node targeting (e.g., adding a CDK4/6 or mTOR inhibitor to PI3Kα inhibitor/fulvestrant backbones). Validation will occur in vivo using patient-derived xenograft (PDX) models with serial cfDNA analysis to monitor clonal dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a clinically actionable map of genomically-aligned drug combinations for ER+ metastatic breast cancer, directly informing next-phase basket trials. It will also provide a functional validation framework for resistance mechanisms predicted by in silico network models, moving the field from biomarker identification to proactive combinatorial therapeutic strategy.
proposals.novelty: While single-agent biomarker screens are common, this proposal uniquely integrates pre-2026 network model predictions of resistance with empirical, high-throughput combination screening across defined genomic strata to design context-specific combination therapies, a critical step towards personalized combinatorial regimens.

proposals.title: Systematic Characterization of Resistance Mechanisms as the Dual of Sensitivity Biomarkers in ER+ Metastatic Breast Cancer
proposals.motivation: In precision oncology, identifying biomarkers of sensitivity (e.g., PTEN loss for AKT inhibitors) has advanced patient selection, but intrinsic and acquired resistance remains a major clinical challenge. A systematic, dual framework to predict resistance mechanisms for each drug-genotype pair is lacking, limiting the durability of targeted therapies like fulvestrant and PI3K/AKT inhibitors in ER+ breast cancer. This gap hinders the development of proactive combination strategies to overcome resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: For a given drug-genotype pair associated with clinical sensitivity, a distinct and predictable set of genomic, transcriptomic, and network-based features will be associated with primary and adaptive resistance, forming a mechanistic 'dual' to the sensitivity signature.
proposals.method: We will integrate pre-treatment and serial cfDNA NGS data from patients on trials like the capivasertib-fulvestrant study to identify genomic correlates of resistance. Using patient-derived xenografts and isogenic cell lines with PTEN/PIK3CA mutations, we will perform CRISPR screens under drug selection to map resistance genes. Network models, built using pre-2026 tools like ARACNe and VIPER, will infer transcription factor and pathway activity from RNA-seq data of resistant models to identify non-genomic adaptive programs.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a paired map of sensitivity and resistance biomarkers for key drug-genotype pairs in ER+ breast cancer, directly informing the design of next-line therapies and rational drug combinations. It will provide a computational-experimental framework to prospectively identify resistance mechanisms, moving the field beyond reactive characterization.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies identify resistance mechanisms in isolation, this proposal formally frames resistance as the systematic dual of sensitivity, using integrated multi-omic and network analyses on well-defined genotype-drug pairs to predict rather than merely describe treatment failure.

proposals.title: Predicting Intrinsic and Acquired Resistance to Endocrine Therapy in ER+ Metastatic Breast Cancer via Pre-Treatment Genomic and Transcriptomic Network Analysis
proposals.motivation: In ER+ metastatic breast cancer, resistance to standard endocrine therapies like fulvestrant remains a major clinical challenge. While genomic studies often focus on matching targetable mutations (e.g., PIK3CA, AKT1) to specific inhibitors, a pre-emptive framework to predict which baseline genomic features will confer resistance to the standard-of-care backbone therapy is lacking. This leads to treatment failure and disease progression before targeted combination strategies can be effectively deployed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pre-treatment genomic alterations and their associated dysregulated signaling network states, derived from tumor and serial cfDNA sequencing, can predict intrinsic and early-acquired resistance to fulvestrant in ER+ metastatic breast cancer, independent of canonical driver mutations.
proposals.method: We will analyze pre-treatment and on-treatment samples from a cohort of patients with ER+ mBC treated with fulvestrant +/- capivasertib (leveraging existing trial data). Using whole-exome and targeted cfDNA sequencing (e.g., Guardant360), we will identify mutations, copy number alterations, and mutational signatures. We will integrate this with baseline RNA-seq to construct patient-specific network models using algorithms like PARADIGM or Influence Maximization, building on our prior cell line network models. A machine learning classifier (e.g., Random Forest or regularized Cox regression) will be trained on these multi-omic features to predict progression-free survival and early progression on fulvestrant monotherapy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a clinically actionable classifier to identify patients likely to be resistant to first-line endocrine therapy before treatment begins, enabling earlier escalation to rational combination regimens. It will also elucidate novel non-canonical resistance pathways beyond PTEN/PIK3CA, offering new biological insights and potential drug targets.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the prevailing paradigm from 'matching mutations to drugs' to 'predicting failure of standard therapy,' and uniquely integrates serial cfDNA dynamics with static tumor network models to forecast resistance at baseline.

proposals.title: Targeting FOXC1 in Circulating Tumor Cells to Suppress EMT and Metastasis in PTEN-mutant ER+ Breast Cancer
proposals.motivation: Metastasis remains the primary cause of breast cancer mortality, driven by circulating tumor cells (CTCs) undergoing epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT). Pre-2026 studies, including Dr. Baselga's work on PTEN-mutant ER+ breast cancer, show that PI3K/AKT pathway activation promotes therapy resistance and a metastatic phenotype. FOXC1, a transcription factor implicated in EMT and metastasis, is a potential downstream effector, but its specific role in CTC biology and as a therapeutic target in defined genetic contexts is unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that FOXC1 is a critical mediator of EMT and survival in CTCs from PTEN-mutant ER+ breast tumors, and that its pharmacological inhibition, particularly in combination with fulvestrant, will block EMT, reduce CTC burden, and suppress metastatic dissemination.
proposals.method: We will use patient-derived xenograft (PDX) models from PTEN-mutant ER+ metastatic breast cancer patients. CTCs will be isolated via microfluidic capture (e.g., CTC-iChip) at serial timepoints during treatment with: (1) fulvestrant, (2) a FOXC1 inhibitor (using pre-2026 tool compounds like siRNA or small-molecule probes), and (3) their combination. We will perform single-cell RNA-sequencing on CTCs to construct EMT gene regulatory networks and quantify FOXC1-driven programs. Response will be assessed by CTC count, metastatic burden in organs, and correlation with network model predictions of FOXC1 dependency.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish FOXC1 as a novel, mechanistically-defined therapeutic target to prevent metastasis by directly attacking the CTC reservoir. It will provide a rationale for a new drug combination (FOXC1 inhibition + endocrine therapy) in the PTEN-mutant subset and deliver a predictive, single-cell network signature of FOXC1 dependency for potential clinical translation.
proposals.novelty: While FOXC1's role in metastasis is known, directly targeting it in CTCs as a strategy to block dissemination in a genetically defined (PTEN-mutant), therapy-resistant population is novel. This proposal uniquely integrates CTC analysis, single-cell network modeling, and combination therapy in a context directly aligned with Dr. Baselga's prior clinical and mechanistic studies on PI3K pathway inhibition.

proposals.title: Combined TGF-beta and PI3K/AKT pathway blockade to suppress mesenchymal CTCs and overcome resistance in PTEN-mutant ER+ metastatic breast cancer
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Baselga's work, establishes that PTEN loss and PI3K/AKT pathway activation drive resistance to endocrine therapy (e.g., fulvestrant) in ER+ breast cancer. The epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT), often induced by TGF-beta signaling, is implicated in generating circulating tumor cells (CTCs) with a mesenchymal phenotype, which are associated with metastasis and poor prognosis. However, the role of TGF-beta in driving resistance to PI3K/AKT inhibitors in PTEN-mutant patients and its effect on mesenchymal CTCs remains poorly defined in the clinical setting.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that TGF-beta signaling drives the emergence of mesenchymal CTCs and contributes to clinical resistance in PTEN-mutant ER+ metastatic breast cancer patients treated with fulvestrant and PI3K/AKT inhibitors (e.g., capivasertib), and that co-targeting the TGF-beta pathway will suppress mesenchymal CTC burden and delay disease progression.
proposals.method: We will conduct a phase Ib/II clinical trial in PTEN-mutant ER+ MBC patients progressing on fulvestrant. Patients will receive fulvestrant + capivasertib (AKT inhibitor) +/- a TGF-beta receptor I kinase inhibitor (e.g., galunisertib). Serial blood samples will be collected for CTC enumeration and characterization using the CellSearch® platform (epithelial) and an FDA-cleared mesenchymal CTC assay (based on vimentin/keratin). We will perform prospective genomic analysis via serial cfDNA NGS (using established Guardant360 or similar panels) to track clonal evolution and correlate with CTC phenotypes. Tumor response will be assessed by RECIST 1.1, and progression-free survival (PFS) will be the primary clinical endpoint.
proposals.contribution: This study will directly test the clinical relevance of TGF-beta-mediated EMT in driving resistance to combined endocrine and PI3K/AKT pathway therapy. It will provide one of the first prospective datasets linking serial cfDNA genomics, dynamic mesenchymal CTC burden, and clinical outcomes in a molecularly defined cohort. The results could identify a novel therapeutic combination and a non-invasive biomarker (mesenchymal CTCs) for patient stratification and early detection of resistance.
proposals.novelty: While TGF-beta's role in EMT is known preclinically, its clinical blockade in combination with PI3K/AKT inhibitors has not been prospectively studied in PTEN-mutant breast cancer. The novelty lies in the integrated, serial analysis of cfDNA and phenotypically defined mesenchymal CTCs as correlative biomarkers within a targeted therapy trial, moving beyond static genomic profiling to a dynamic, functional assessment of resistance mechanisms.

proposals.title: Pan-Cancer Analysis of Circulating Tumor Cell Epithelial-Mesenchymal Transition Phenotypes and Their Association with Genomic Alterations and Treatment Response
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Baselga's work, has established that the epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) status of circulating tumor cells (CTCs) in breast cancer is a dynamic biomarker of metastasis and therapy resistance. However, the prevalence, clinical relevance, and genomic correlates of EMT phenotypes in CTCs across major epithelial cancers remain poorly characterized. Validating this biomarker pan-cancer is critical for developing liquid biopsy strategies to guide therapies targeting EMT-related pathways, such as PI3K/AKT.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that EMT phenotypes in CTCs are a universal feature of metastatic progression across lung, prostate, and pancreatic cancers, and that specific EMT states correlate with actionable genomic alterations (e.g., PTEN loss, PIK3CA mutations) and predict differential response to targeted therapies.
proposals.method: We will prospectively collect serial blood samples from patients with metastatic ER+ breast, lung adenocarcinoma, prostate, and pancreatic cancer enrolled on biomarker-driven phase I/II trials (e.g., involving PI3K/AKT inhibitors). CTCs will be isolated using the CellSearch system and a microfluidic Parsortix platform for label-free capture. A multiplexed immunofluorescence assay (pre-2026 panels for epithelial (EpCAM, CK) and mesenchymal (Vimentin, N-cadherin) markers) will classify CTCs into epithelial, hybrid, and mesenchymal phenotypes. This EMT profiling will be integrated with deep, serial cfDNA NGS (using established Guardant360 or FoundationOne Liquid panels) to link CTC phenotypes with tumor genomics and radiographic treatment response.
proposals.contribution: This study will determine the generalizability of EMT-based CTC classification as a pan-cancer biomarker, directly linking CTC phenotypes to underlying driver mutations and clinical outcomes on targeted therapies. It will generate a unique, multi-modal liquid biopsy dataset to inform the development of EMT-directed combination therapies and patient selection strategies beyond breast cancer.
proposals.novelty: While EMT in CTCs has been studied in single cancer types, a systematic, prospective pan-cancer analysis integrated with serial cfDNA genomics in the context of therapeutic trials is novel. This approach moves beyond cataloging phenotypes to actively testing their functional and predictive relevance across diverse malignancies.

proposals.title: Targeting MCL1-Amplified Breast Cancers with Selective Protein-Protein Interaction Inhibitors: A Synthetic Lethality Approach
proposals.motivation: MCL1 amplification is a common resistance mechanism in ER+ breast cancer, particularly following therapies like CDK4/6 inhibitors and PI3K/AKT pathway inhibitors, and confers poor prognosis. Direct MCL1 inhibition with BH3 mimetics faces significant clinical challenges due to on-target toxicities, especially cardiac and hematological. A novel strategy to selectively kill MCL1-dependent cancer cells while sparing normal tissues is urgently needed.
proposals.hypothesis: Cancer cells with MCL1 amplification are synthetically lethal to the disruption of specific, high-affinity MCL1:effector protein interactions (e.g., with NOXA or BIM), and selective small-molecule PPI inhibitors can achieve this disruption, inducing apoptosis with a wider therapeutic window than pan-BH3 mimetics.
proposals.method: We will first characterize the essential MCL1-effector dependencies in isogenic and patient-derived ER+ breast cancer models with and without MCL1 amplification using co-immunoprecipitation and CRISPR-based functional screens. Leveraging the structural biology of the MCL1:NOXA/BIM interfaces, we will employ a fragment-based screening and structure-guided design campaign to develop selective PPI inhibitors. Candidate compounds will be tested in vitro and in vivo using PTEN-mutant and other relevant models from our prior work, with efficacy and toxicity assessed via serial cfDNA analysis and PDX studies to correlate response with MCL1 amplification status.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver first-in-class, mechanistically distinct probes targeting MCL1 via co-factor disruption, providing a new therapeutic strategy for MCL1-amplified, treatment-resistant breast cancers. It will establish the synthetic lethality of specific MCL1 PPIs as a targetable vulnerability, potentially defining a biomarker-driven patient population for future clinical trials.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in adapting an allosteric PPI disruption strategy from antiviral drug discovery (e.g., LEDGINs) to an oncogenic dependency, moving beyond direct active-site inhibition. It focuses on selective effector disruption rather than broad MCL1 blockade, a concept not yet clinically explored in oncology.

proposals.title: Predicting and Overcoming Resistance to Selective MCL1 Inhibitors in PTEN-mutant ER+ Breast Cancer
proposals.motivation: MCL1 amplification is a common genomic event in breast cancer, particularly in aggressive subtypes, and drives resistance to standard therapies by promoting cell survival. While selective MCL1 inhibitors (e.g., S63845, AMG 176) are in early clinical development, mechanisms of de novo and acquired resistance in genetically defined contexts remain poorly understood. This is critical for patient selection and combination strategies, especially in PTEN-mutant ER+ breast cancer, a population with high unmet need and known reliance on parallel survival pathways like PI3K/AKT.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that PTEN-mutant, ER+ breast cancers with MCL1 amplification will demonstrate initial sensitivity to selective MCL1 inhibitors, but will develop resistance through the upregulation of compensatory anti-apoptotic BCL-2 family proteins and/or activation of parallel survival signaling pathways, which can be preemptively targeted with rational drug combinations.
proposals.method: We will utilize a panel of PTEN-mutant ER+ breast cancer cell lines and patient-derived xenografts (PDXs) with known MCL1 amplification status. Sensitivity to MCL1 inhibitors (e.g., S63845) will be assessed via dose-response assays and correlated with baseline multi-omics profiles (RNA-seq, RPPA). Resistance models will be generated via chronic drug exposure. Dynamic changes in apoptotic signaling and pathway activation will be monitored using serial cfDNA NGS (tracking clonal evolution) and phospho-proteomics. Network models of ER+ signaling, akin to prior PI3Kα inhibitor studies, will be built using pre-2026 tools (Cytoscape, gene regulatory network inference) to identify candidate resistance nodes for combination therapy, which will be validated in vitro and in vivo.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the molecular determinants of response and resistance to MCL1 inhibitors in a genomically defined breast cancer subset, directly informing clinical trial design. It will generate predictive biomarkers and rational, biomarker-driven combination strategies (e.g., MCL1i + AKT inhibitor) to overcome resistance, extending the therapeutic utility of this novel drug class. The integrated use of serial cfDNA and network modeling provides a translational framework applicable to other targeted agents.
proposals.novelty: While MCL1 dependency in amplified cancers is known, this proposal uniquely focuses on the understudied context of PTEN-mutant ER+ disease and employs a dynamic, multi-modal approach—integrating serial liquid biopsy with cell line-specific network modeling—to prospectively identify and target emergent resistance mechanisms, moving beyond static biomarker discovery.

proposals.title: Pan-Cancer Somatic Copy Number Alteration (SCNA) Landscapes Reveal Convergent Dependencies and Biomarkers for PI3K/AKT Pathway Inhibition
proposals.motivation: Somatic copy number alterations (SCNAs) are pervasive drivers of cancer, yet their patterns across cancer types are not fully exploited to identify shared therapeutic vulnerabilities. Pre-2026, large-scale genomic projects like TCGA have catalogued SCNAs, but integrative analyses linking specific cross-cancer SCNA patterns to functional dependencies and drug response, particularly for agents like PI3K/AKT inhibitors, are lacking. This represents a missed opportunity to develop pan-cancer biomarkers for targeted therapies, especially in cancers like ER+ breast cancer where PTEN loss and PI3K pathway activation are common.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that recurrent, convergent SCNA patterns across distinct cancer types delineate common oncogenic dependencies, and that these shared SCNA signatures can predict sensitivity to specific pathway-targeted therapies, such as PI3K/AKT inhibitors.
proposals.method: We will analyze SCNA data (logR values) from pre-2026 TCGA and other public cohorts across multiple cancer types using GISTIC 2.0 to identify significant, recurrent arm- and focal-level events. We will apply non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) to deconvolve these events into latent SCNA signatures. These signatures will be correlated with in vitro drug response data (e.g., GDSC, CTRP) and functional genomic screens (e.g., CRISPR-Cas9 dependency maps) to link patterns to vulnerabilities. Finally, we will validate predictions in patient-derived models and via retrospective analysis of clinical trial data, such as from PTEN-mutant ER+ breast cancer cohorts treated with capivasertib and fulvestrant.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a novel, functionally annotated map of pan-cancer SCNA signatures, moving beyond tissue-of-origin classification. It will nominate new biomarker candidates for PI3K/AKT pathway inhibitors based on SCNA patterns, potentially expanding their use beyond canonical mutations. The resource will provide a framework for identifying patient subsets across cancers that may benefit from the same targeted therapy.
proposals.novelty: While SCNA patterns are well-described within cancer types, this proposal uniquely seeks to define and functionally validate *convergent* SCNA patterns *across* cancers as predictors of shared drug vulnerability, integrating computational deconvolution with pre-clinical and clinical validation in a pathway-focused manner.

proposals.title: A Bayesian Topic Model of Somatic Copy Number Alterations Predicts Clinical Benefit from Trastuzumab in HER2-Non-Amplified Metastatic Breast Cancer
proposals.motivation: Current HER2-positive breast cancer classification relies on IHC and FISH, but clinical data show a spectrum of benefit from HER2-targeted therapies, including in IHC 2+/FISH+ or borderline cases. This suggests underlying genomic heterogeneity not captured by current binary thresholds. Pre-2026, somatic copy number alteration (SCNA) signatures have been linked to tumor biology and outcomes in other contexts, but a probabilistic, data-driven framework to link SCNA patterns to HER2 therapy response is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: Latent SCNA signatures, derived via Bayesian topic modeling, define genomically distinct subgroups within tumors with non-canonical HER2 status (IHC 2+/FISH+ or low-level amplification) that are predictive of clinical benefit from trastuzumab-based therapy.
proposals.method: We will apply a hierarchical Dirichlet process (HDP) model, analogous to latent Dirichlet allocation for text, to deconvolve whole-genome or high-density array SCNA profiles from pretreatment tumor biopsies into latent 'SCNA topics' representing co-amplified/deleted genomic regions. Using a cohort of metastatic breast cancer patients treated with trastuzumab (with known IHC/FISH status and clinical benefit data), we will build a Bayesian logistic regression model where the probability of clinical benefit (RECIST response or PFS >6 months) is a function of the inferred topic proportions, controlling for clinical covariates. Model inference will be performed using Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative genomic classifier to refine patient selection for HER2-targeted agents beyond current IHC/FISH cut-offs. It will identify specific co-alteration signatures associated with trastuzumab sensitivity or resistance, offering biological insights into response heterogeneity. The probabilistic output offers a clinically interpretable measure of predicted benefit.
proposals.novelty: While SCNA analysis is established, applying Bayesian topic modeling specifically to predict HER2 therapy response in non-amplified populations is novel. This approach moves beyond single-gene HER2 assessment to model the predictive power of the entire SCNA landscape, fusing computational genomics with direct clinical translation.

proposals.title: A Pan-Cancer Atlas of Rare, Recurrent Somatic Copy Number Alterations from >100,000 Tumors
proposals.motivation: Prior large-scale analyses, like TCGA Pan-Cancer Atlas of ~10,000 tumors, have catalogued common SCNAs but lack power to detect rare, recurrent events. These rare alterations may define clinically meaningful molecular subsets and reveal new therapeutic vulnerabilities, particularly in cancers like ER+ breast cancer where resistance to targeted agents like PI3K/AKT inhibitors (e.g., capivasertib) is common. Scaling to 100,000+ specimens is now feasible due to public genomic data aggregation and can address this critical gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that scaling SCNA analysis to over 100,000 tumors will reveal novel, statistically significant rare recurrent SCNAs that are cancer-type specific, associate with specific mutational backgrounds (e.g., PTEN loss), and may inform mechanisms of de novo or acquired resistance to targeted therapies.
proposals.method: We will aggregate and uniformly process SNP array and whole-exome sequencing data from >100,000 tumors across public repositories (TCGA, ICGC, GENIE) and institutional cohorts. SCNAs will be called using GISTIC 2.0 and FACETS for array and sequencing data, respectively. We will perform pan-cancer and cancer-type-specific analyses to identify arm-level and focal SCNAs, using rigorous false-discovery rate control. Associations with mutations (e.g., PTEN, PIK3CA) and clinical data will be tested, with functional validation guided by network models of drug response.
proposals.contribution: This will produce the largest, most comprehensive map of rare recurrent SCNAs in human cancer, a resource for the community. It will nominate new candidate driver loci and genomic contexts for therapy resistance, directly informing the design of future basket trials and combination strategies, such as those involving fulvestrant and AKT inhibitors.
proposals.novelty: The scale of >100,000 tumors represents an order-of-magnitude increase over prior pan-cancer SCNA studies, providing unprecedented statistical power to discover rare events and integrate them with mutational and therapeutic response data from precision oncology studies.

proposals.title: A Phase II Basket Study of Fulvestrant plus Capivasertib in PTEN-Altered, ER-Positive Solid Tumors: A Tissue-Agnostic Approach
proposals.motivation: The success of tissue-agnostic therapies targeting NTRK fusions or MSI-high status demonstrates that molecular alterations can supersede tumor histology in predicting drug response. However, this paradigm is largely untested for key oncogenic drivers in hormone receptor-positive cancers, such as PTEN loss, which is a known predictor of sensitivity to AKT inhibition in ER+ breast cancer. Extending this biomarker-driven strategy to other PTEN-mutant, ER-positive solid tumors (e.g., endometrial, ovarian) could unlock effective treatments for patients with rare or refractory cancers lacking standard options.
proposals.hypothesis: The combination of fulvestrant (ER antagonist) and capivasertib (AKT inhibitor) will demonstrate clinically meaningful anti-tumor activity in patients with PTEN-altered, ER-positive metastatic solid tumors of any primary site, with efficacy comparable to that observed in PTEN-mutant ER+ breast cancer.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, open-label, phase II basket trial. Eligible patients will have metastatic, ER-positive (≥1% by IHC), PTEN-altered (loss by IHC and/or inactivating mutation by NGS) solid tumors, grouped into baskets by tumor type (e.g., breast, endometrial, ovarian, other). The primary endpoint is objective response rate (ORR) per RECIST 1.1 within each basket. We will employ serial cfDNA NGS (using established panels like Guardant360) at baseline and progression to monitor clonal evolution and identify genomic mechanisms of resistance, as done in prior Baselga team studies. Pre-clinical validation will use existing ER+ breast cancer cell line network models to computationally predict and then test in vitro the efficacy of the combination in non-breast ER+ PTEN-null cell lines.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first clinical evidence for a tissue-agnostic, biomarker-defined approach combining endocrine therapy and AKT inhibition. It will establish the clinical utility of PTEN/ER co-alteration as a predictive biomarker across tumor types and generate a rich dataset on the genomic evolution of resistance in this context, informing future combination strategies.
proposals.novelty: While PTEN loss is a known breast cancer biomarker, applying it as a tissue-agnostic criterion for a combination therapy targeting both the ER and PI3K/AKT pathways is novel. This moves beyond histology to define a patient population by a specific molecular phenotype (ER+/PTEN-altered), a strategy not yet prospectively tested for this drug combination.

proposals.title: Integrating Germline and Somatic Variant Data to Predict Treatment Response and Resistance in ER+ Metastatic Breast Cancer
proposals.motivation: Current precision oncology frameworks, including those for ER+ breast cancer, primarily focus on somatic mutations to guide therapy selection. However, germline variants in genes like BRCA1/2, PTEN, and CHEK2 are known to influence cancer predisposition and may also modulate tumor biology and therapeutic response. A comprehensive, clinically annotated knowledge base linking hereditary predisposition variants to treatment outcomes in the metastatic setting is lacking, limiting a holistic view of patient stratification.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific germline cancer predisposition variants, particularly those affecting DNA repair and PI3K/AKT pathway genes, interact with somatic alterations to influence clinical outcomes and resistance mechanisms to standard therapies (e.g., fulvestrant) and targeted agents (e.g., PI3Kα/AKT inhibitors) in ER+ metastatic breast cancer.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing patient cohorts from our prior trials (e.g., capivasertib + fulvestrant in PTEN-mutant tumors) and expand with prospective germline sequencing (using targeted NGS panels for hereditary cancer genes). We will integrate germline variant calls with matched tumor somatic profiling (from tissue and serial cfDNA) and clinical response data. Statistical analyses (multivariate Cox regression, logistic regression) and network modeling approaches (extending our prior cell line-specific ER+ network models) will be used to identify germline-somatic interaction signatures predictive of progression-free survival and differential drug response.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first clinically annotated resource linking germline genetics to treatment outcomes in ER+ metastatic breast cancer. It will provide actionable insights for patient stratification beyond somatic profiling alone and may reveal novel biological mechanisms of de novo or acquired resistance influenced by germline background.
proposals.novelty: While germline testing is standard for hereditary risk assessment, its systematic integration with somatic evolution and therapy response data in metastatic breast cancer to inform real-time treatment decisions is a novel and unexplored translational approach.

proposals.title: A Clinically Annotated Knowledge Base of Resistance Mechanisms and Rational Combinations for ER+ Metastatic Breast Cancer Following Targeted Therapy Failure
proposals.motivation: The sequential use of targeted therapies like PI3K/AKT pathway inhibitors (e.g., capivasertib) with endocrine therapy (e.g., fulvestrant) has improved outcomes in ER+ metastatic breast cancer (MBC), particularly in PTEN-mutant tumors. However, acquired resistance is inevitable, and the mechanisms are heterogeneous. Clinicians lack a structured, evidence-based resource to guide the next rational therapeutic combination after initial targeted therapy failure, leading to suboptimal treatment sequencing.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematic integration of preclinical and clinical genomic data will reveal recurrent, targetable resistance networks to first-line PI3K/AKT/endocrine combinations, and that annotation of these networks with rational next-line drug combinations will generate a clinically actionable decision-support framework.
proposals.method: We will first curate a comprehensive dataset from pre-2026 sources: 1) genomic and outcome data from our and published trials (e.g., FAKTION, CAPItello-291), 2) resistance mechanisms from serial cfDNA studies and cell line network models (e.g., ESR1 mutations, RB1 loss, feedback activation), and 3) drug combination screens from PDX and cell line studies. We will then apply a knowledge graph architecture, linking mutations (e.g., PTEN, PIK3CA, ESR1) to resistance phenotypes and annotated drug combinations (e.g., CDK4/6 + mTOR inhibitors post-AKTi). The graph will be queried via a rule-based inference engine to propose context-specific next-line regimens.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, publicly accessible knowledge base that moves beyond static mutation-drug associations to dynamic, mechanism-based treatment sequencing. It will provide a testable framework for personalizing therapy after progression on first-line targeted combinations in ER+ MBC, directly informing the design of next-generation adaptive clinical trials.
proposals.novelty: While resistance mechanisms are studied in isolation, this project uniquely integrates disparate clinical trial data, serial cfDNA analyses, and computational network models into a unified, annotated resource specifically designed to guide clinical decision-making for the critical post-progression setting.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of Histology-Specific Tumor Mutational Burden (TMB) Cutpoints for Immunotherapy Response in Metastatic Breast Cancer Subtypes
proposals.motivation: As of 2025, tumor mutational burden (TMB) is emerging as a biomarker for immune checkpoint inhibitor (ICI) response, but a universal TMB-high cutpoint (e.g., 10 mut/Mb) lacks specificity across cancer types. In breast cancer, particularly hormone receptor-positive (HR+) disease, TMB is generally lower, and the clinical utility of a pan-cancer threshold is unclear. This imprecision may incorrectly exclude patients with lower, but potentially meaningful, tumor-specific TMB levels from immunotherapy trials or treatment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that defining histology-specific TMB thresholds, stratified by breast cancer subtype (e.g., HR+/HER2-, triple-negative), will more accurately identify patients who benefit from immunotherapy than the current universal cutpoint, particularly in genomically defined subsets like PTEN-mutant tumors.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing genomic and clinical data from our prior trials (e.g., capivasertib + fulvestrant in PTEN-mutant ER+ mBC) and public cohorts (TCGA, METABRIC) with linked outcomes. Using prospectively collected serial cfDNA NGS data from our phase I basket study, we will calculate TMB. We will apply receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis and maximally selected rank statistics to determine optimal TMB cutpoints for objective response rate (ORR) and progression-free survival (PFS) within each histologic and molecular subtype. Validation will be performed in an independent, held-out clinical cohort.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a refined, clinically actionable framework for using TMB in breast cancer immunotherapy selection. It will directly inform the design of future biomarker-stratified trials combining ICIs with targeted agents (e.g., PI3K/AKT inhibitors) in HR+ disease. The study will also demonstrate the utility of serial cfDNA for dynamic biomarker assessment.
proposals.novelty: While TMB is broadly studied, the development of validated, cancer-type-specific thresholds for breast cancer, integrated with mutational profiles (e.g., PTEN, PIK3CA) and serial liquid biopsy data, represents a precision oncology approach beyond the one-size-fits-all model prevalent pre-2026.

proposals.title: A Composite Biomarker Model Integrating TMB, PD-L1, MSI, and Immune Infiltrate to Predict Response to Immune Checkpoint Inhibition in Metastatic ER+ Breast Cancer
proposals.motivation: While immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) show promise, predictive biomarkers like Tumor Mutational Burden (TMB) or PD-L1 alone have limited sensitivity and specificity in ER+ metastatic breast cancer (MBC). This leads to many patients receiving ineffective, costly, and potentially toxic therapies. A multi-modal biomarker approach, grounded in the complex tumor-immune microenvironment, is urgently needed to better select patients most likely to benefit from immunotherapy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a composite biomarker score, integrating quantitative measures of TMB, PD-L1 expression, MSI status, and specific immune cell infiltrates (e.g., CD8+ T cells, Tregs), will outperform any single biomarker in predicting objective response and progression-free survival to ICI therapy in patients with ER+ MBC.
proposals.method: We will leverage serial cfDNA and tumor biopsy samples from a prospective cohort of ER+ MBC patients treated with ICIs (e.g., pembrolizumab) +/- targeted therapy (e.g., fulvestrant, PI3K/AKT inhibitors). TMB and MSI will be assessed from targeted NGS panels. PD-L1 expression will be quantified via combined positive score (CPS). Digital pathology (H&E and multiplex immunofluorescence) will quantify spatial immune infiltrates. A multivariate logistic regression model, followed by machine learning approaches (e.g., random forest or elastic net regression), will be used to integrate these features into a composite predictive score, validated in an independent cohort.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a clinically actionable, validated composite biomarker for ICI response in ER+ MBC, directly addressing a critical unmet need in precision oncology. It will provide a mechanistic framework linking genomic instability, adaptive immune resistance, and the local immune contexture. The findings could guide more rational patient selection for immunotherapy trials and combination strategies in this population.
proposals.novelty: This study moves beyond assessing biomarkers in isolation by creating an integrated, biologically grounded model specifically for ER+ breast cancer, a subtype where immunotherapy biomarkers are poorly defined. It uniquely incorporates serial cfDNA analysis to track biomarker evolution under therapeutic pressure from targeted agents, a key consideration in this patient population.

proposals.title: Prospective, Randomized Validation of Tumor Mutational Burden as a Predictive Biomarker for Immunotherapy Benefit in PTEN-mutant, Hormone Receptor-Positive Metastatic Breast Cancer
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, high tumor mutational burden (TMB) has been associated with improved response to immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) in retrospective analyses across several cancers, including subsets of breast cancer. However, these associations require prospective validation to establish TMB as a reliable predictive biomarker for clinical use. This is particularly urgent in hormone receptor-positive (HR+) metastatic breast cancer (MBC), where PTEN mutations are linked to PI3K/AKT pathway activation, endocrine resistance, and potentially a more immunogenic microenvironment, creating a rational combination strategy.
proposals.hypothesis: In patients with PTEN-mutant, HR+ MBC, high TMB (≥10 mut/Mb) prospectively identifies a subgroup that derives significant progression-free and overall survival benefit from the addition of an anti-PD-L1 antibody to standard fulvestrant and capivasertib therapy, compared to those with low TMB.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multicenter, randomized Phase II trial. Patients with PTEN-mutant, ER+ MBC will be prospectively stratified by central TMB assessment (FoundationOne CDx or similar NGS panel) into High (≥10 mut/Mb) and Low (<10 mut/Mb) cohorts. Both cohorts will be randomized 1:1 to receive fulvestrant + capivasertib with or without durvalumab (anti-PD-L1). The primary endpoint will be progression-free survival (PFS) in the High-TMB cohort. Serial cfDNA collection, as used in Baselga's prior work, will enable dynamic monitoring of clonal evolution and resistance mechanisms via NGS. Statistical analysis will use a stratified log-rank test for the primary comparison.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first prospective, level 1 evidence for TMB as a predictive biomarker in HR+ breast cancer, directly informing clinical trial design and potential regulatory pathways. It will elucidate the interaction between PTEN loss, PI3K/AKT inhibition, and the tumor immune microenvironment, guiding rational drug combinations. The serial cfDNA analysis will offer insights into the dynamics of TMB and resistance under therapeutic pressure.
proposals.novelty: While TMB has been studied retrospectively, this is a prospective, biomarker-stratified trial specifically in a molecularly defined (PTEN-mutant), immunotherapy-resistant breast cancer subtype. It uniquely tests the synergistic potential of combining AKT inhibition (capivasertib) with immunotherapy based on a mechanistically grounded biomarker hypothesis, moving beyond generic ICI trials.

proposals.title: A Pan-Cancer, Multi-Cohort Meta-Analysis of Tumor Mutational Burden and Overall Survival: Validation and Refinement of Predictive Biomarkers
proposals.motivation: Tumor mutational burden (TMB) has emerged as a potential biomarker for immunotherapy response, but its association with overall survival across cancer types remains inconsistently defined, with studies limited by cohort size and heterogeneity. A robust, large-scale validation is critical to establish TMB as a reliable prognostic and predictive tool for clinical decision-making, particularly in guiding combination strategies with targeted agents like PI3K/AKT inhibitors in relevant genomic subsets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a meta-analysis of tens of thousands of patients will reveal robust, cancer-type-specific TMB-survival associations, and that within high-TMB populations, specific co-mutation patterns (e.g., PTEN loss) will identify subgroups with differential outcomes, informing rational therapeutic combinations.
proposals.method: We will aggregate and harmonize clinical and whole-exome or targeted NGS data from >50,000 patients across international consortia (e.g., GENIE, TCGA) and industry-sponsored trials. Survival analyses (Cox proportional hazards models) will be performed within and across cancer types, adjusting for key covariates. We will employ machine learning (regularized regression, random forests) to identify genomic co-alterations (e.g., PTEN, PIK3CA) that modify the TMB-survival relationship, building on our prior network modeling approaches.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the most definitive pre-2026 evidence on the prognostic value of TMB pan-cancer. It will generate a publicly available resource for biomarker discovery and validate TMB thresholds for clinical use. Furthermore, it will identify specific mutational contexts where high TMB may synergize with or predict resistance to targeted therapies, directly informing trial design for combinations like AKT inhibitors + immunotherapy.
proposals.novelty: The scale (>50k patients) and multi-cohort design address the major limitations of prior studies. The analysis moves beyond a simple TMB-survival correlation to mechanistically dissect how specific driver mutations (a focus of Dr. Baselga's work) interact with TMB to influence tumor biology and therapeutic vulnerability.

proposals.title: Continuous HER2 Quantification by Digital Pathology and RNA-Seq to Predict Response to Dual HER2 Blockade in Metastatic Breast Cancer
proposals.motivation: Current HER2-positive/negative binary classification, based on IHC and FISH, is a cornerstone of treatment selection but is an oversimplification. A significant proportion of patients with 'HER2-low' or heterogeneous expression do not respond optimally to HER2-targeted therapies, particularly dual blockade with trastuzumab and pertuzumab. A continuous, quantitative measure of HER2 could better capture biological heterogeneity and improve patient selection, maximizing efficacy and sparing toxicity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a continuous, multi-modal quantification of HER2 expression (protein and mRNA) will correlate more strongly with clinical response to dual HER2 blockade than binary classification, identifying a subset of patients with intermediate expression levels who derive significant benefit.
proposals.method: We will leverage a cohort of ER+/HER2+ metastatic breast cancer patients treated with dual blockade from prior studies. HER2 will be quantified continuously using: 1) Digital image analysis of archival tumor IHC slides (e.g., Halo, QuPath) to generate H-scores, and 2) RNA-seq data to derive HER2 (ERBB2) transcript levels. These continuous variables will be integrated with clinical response data (RECIST) and serial cfDNA NGS (tracking mutations like PIK3CA, PTEN) using multivariate regression models. We will build a predictive model using a penalized Cox regression (LASSO) for progression-free survival.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a proof-of-concept framework for moving beyond binary biomarker classification in breast cancer. It will deliver a validated, quantitative HER2 scoring method that can be tested prospectively for patient stratification. Furthermore, by integrating genomic data (e.g., PTEN status), it may reveal co-alterations that modulate response within defined HER2 expression ranges.
proposals.novelty: While HER2-low is a recognized entity, systematically replacing the clinical binary cutoff with a continuous predictor for dual blockade selection is novel. The integration of digital pathology quantification with RNA-seq and serial cfDNA analysis, building on Dr. Baselga's expertise in PTEN-mutant contexts and basket study genomics, provides a uniquely comprehensive approach.

proposals.title: Overcoming Compensatory HER3 Signaling in ER+/HER2- Metastatic Breast Cancer: A Phase Ib Study of Patritumab deruxtecan (HER3-DXd) Combined with Fulvestrant and Capivasertib in PTEN-mutant Tumors
proposals.motivation: In ER+/HER2- metastatic breast cancer (MBC), PTEN loss/PIK3CA mutations drive resistance to endocrine therapy via PI3K/AKT signaling. While AKT inhibitors (e.g., capivasertib) + fulvestrant show efficacy, preclinical models indicate that PI3K/AKT inhibition can trigger compensatory HER3 activation, a known resistance mechanism. This adaptive signaling may limit the durability of response to dual therapy, particularly in PTEN-mutant tumors which are highly PI3K-pathway dependent.
proposals.hypothesis: Adding the anti-HER3 antibody-drug conjugate patritumab deruxtecan (HER3-DXd) to fulvestrant and capivasertib will prevent this compensatory HER3 signaling, leading to deeper and more durable tumor responses in patients with PTEN-mutant, ER+/HER2- MBC.
proposals.method: We will conduct a single-arm Phase Ib study in patients with PTEN-mutant ER+/HER2- MBC progressing on endocrine therapy. Patients will receive the triple combination. Primary endpoints are safety and recommended phase II dose. Serial tumor biopsies (pre-treatment and C1D15) and longitudinal cfDNA collection will be used for dynamic biomarker analysis. We will employ targeted NGS (Guardant360, FoundationOne Liquid CDx) on cfDNA to track genomic evolution and PTEN allelic fraction. Phospho-protein signaling networks in biopsies will be analyzed via reverse-phase protein arrays (RPPA) to quantify suppression of pAKT and pERK and assess residual HER3/PI3K pathway activity.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first clinical safety and preliminary efficacy data for a triple HER-family/PI3K-pathway blockade strategy in this genomic subset. It will mechanistically define the role of HER3 signaling as an adaptive resistance pathway to AKT inhibition in patients and validate cfDNA as a dynamic tool for monitoring PTEN status and emergent resistance.
proposals.novelty: While dual HER2/HER3 blockade has been explored in HER2+ cancer, applying a HER3-ADC to prevent feedback signaling in HER2-negative, PTEN-mutant ER+ breast cancer combined with an AKTi is a novel therapeutic concept. The study uniquely integrates serial cfDNA and proteomic network analysis from patient samples, a method refined in the researcher's recent work, to directly test the mechanism of action.

proposals.title: A Randomized Phase II Study of Short-Duration vs. Standard-Duration Trastuzumab in Node-Negative, HER2-Positive Early Breast Cancer Guided by ctDNA Dynamics
proposals.motivation: The standard one-year adjuvant trastuzumab regimen for HER2+ breast cancer is applied uniformly, despite significant heterogeneity in recurrence risk, particularly between node-negative and node-positive patients. This exposes lower-risk patients to unnecessary cardiotoxicity and cost, while potentially undertreating higher-risk subsets. In the pre-2026 era, de-escalation strategies are being explored, but lack robust, real-time biomarkers to guide patient-specific duration.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that node-negative, HER2+ early breast cancer patients with undetectable circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) after a short (e.g., 3-6 month) course of trastuzumab will have non-inferior outcomes compared to those receiving the standard 12-month regimen, whereas node-positive patients will require the full duration or combination strategies for optimal disease control.
proposals.method: We propose a multicenter, randomized phase II trial for stage I-IIA (node-negative) HER2+ breast cancer, comparing 6 months vs. 12 months of adjuvant trastuzumab. Serial plasma samples will be collected for ctDNA analysis using a patient-specific, tumor-informed multiplex PCR assay (e.g., Safe-SeqS or similar NGS-based method) at baseline, post-surgery, and every 3 months during therapy. The primary endpoint is ctDNA clearance rate and persistence. A parallel, observational cohort of node-positive patients will undergo identical ctDNA monitoring on standard therapy to identify molecular patterns of early resistance, informing future combination strategies (e.g., with PI3K/AKT inhibitors).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first prospective evidence linking ctDNA dynamics to a de-escalated trastuzumab duration, potentially defining a molecularly-guided treatment paradigm. It will generate a rich dataset on the genomic evolution of HER2+ disease under therapeutic pressure, identifying early resistance mechanisms in different risk groups.
proposals.novelty: While ctDNA is known to be prognostic, its use as a real-time, dynamic decision-making tool for modulating the duration of a targeted therapy in the adjuvant setting is novel. The study uniquely integrates a randomized de-escalation design with deep serial genomic monitoring to move beyond static clinicopathological criteria.

proposals.title: A Prospective, Randomized Biomarker-Integrated Study of One-Year Versus Two-Year Adjuvant Trastuzumab in HER2-Positive Early Breast Cancer: Efficacy, Toxicity, and Genomic Correlates
proposals.motivation: The standard one-year duration of adjuvant trastuzumab for HER2-positive early breast cancer was established by historical trials, but the optimal treatment length remains a critical clinical and economic question. A longer duration could potentially improve disease-free survival (DFS) but may increase cardiac toxicity and financial burden. In the pre-2026 era of precision oncology, a refined understanding of which patients benefit from extended therapy is needed, particularly in the context of co-occurring genomic alterations like PIK3CA or PTEN mutations that may influence response.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a two-year duration of adjuvant trastuzumab will not provide a superior DFS benefit over one year for the overall population but will identify a molecular subset of patients (e.g., those with specific baseline or emergent mutations in the PI3K/AKT pathway) who derive significant clinical benefit from the extended treatment.
proposals.method: We propose a multicenter, randomized Phase III trial comparing 1 vs. 2 years of adjuvant trastuzumab. The primary endpoint is 5-year DFS. Key secondary endpoints include overall survival, cardiac toxicity (LVEF monitoring), and cost-effectiveness. Leveraging Dr. Baselga's recent methodological focus, we will integrate prospective serial genomic analysis. Using next-generation sequencing (NGS) of tumor tissue (baseline) and serial circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) at predefined intervals, we will model response and resistance. We will specifically analyze mutations in PIK3CA, PTEN, and ESR1, and employ network models of ER+/HER2+ signaling to identify biomarkers predictive of differential benefit from treatment duration.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide definitive evidence on the optimal duration of a cornerstone HER2-targeted therapy. It will generate a rich, longitudinal genomic dataset to identify which patients are most likely to benefit from extended therapy and which are not, thereby moving towards a more personalized treatment strategy. The findings will directly impact global clinical guidelines and healthcare resource utilization.
proposals.novelty: While prior trials (e.g., HERA, PHARE) addressed duration, this study uniquely integrates prospective, serial ctDNA-based genomic monitoring and pathway-specific network modeling from the outset to discover predictive biomarkers, moving beyond a simple one-size-fits-all comparison.

proposals.title: Genomic and Clinical Correlates of Long-Term Cardiac Dysfunction in HER2-Positive Breast Cancer Survivors Treated with Adjuvant Trastuzumab
proposals.motivation: Adjuvant trastuzumab has dramatically improved outcomes for HER2-positive breast cancer, but its long-term cardiac safety profile remains incompletely defined, with most trials reporting median follow-up of only 1-2 years. As survival increases, understanding the incidence, risk factors, and molecular drivers of late-onset cardiotoxicity is critical for the lifelong care of a growing survivor population. This aligns with Dr. Baselga's focus on patient outcomes and precision oncology in breast cancer.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a subset of patients will develop late-onset, subclinical cardiac dysfunction more than 5 years post-treatment, and that this risk is associated with specific germline genetic variants and somatic tumor mutations (e.g., in PIK3CA, PTEN) that modulate the HER2/PI3K/AKT signaling axis in cardiomyocytes.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective-prospective cohort study of patients from Dr. Baselga's prior adjuvant trastuzumab trials, extending follow-up with serial echocardiograms and cardiac biomarker (hs-TnT, NT-proBNP) assessments. Germline DNA will be analyzed using a targeted NGS panel of cardiac risk and PI3K/AKT pathway genes. We will correlate longitudinal cardiac function data with genomic features using mixed-effects models. Somatic mutation data from original tumor biopsies (e.g., PTEN loss, PIK3CA mutations) will be integrated to explore tumor-intrinsic factors influencing treatment duration and potential cardiac risk.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed genomic landscape associated with long-term trastuzumab cardiotoxicity, moving beyond clinical risk factors. It will establish evidence-based guidelines for the duration and intensity of cardiac surveillance in survivors. Furthermore, it may identify high-risk genotypes, enabling pre-emptive strategies or personalized adjuvant therapy selection.
proposals.novelty: While acute cardiotoxicity is well-studied, the systematic genomic analysis of late-onset effects beyond 5 years is novel. Integrating germline genetics with original tumor somatic profiles to inform cardiac risk represents a unique, precision medicine approach to a classic oncology survivorship problem.

proposals.title: Overall Survival Analysis of Capivasertib plus Fulvestrant in PTEN-Altered ER+ Metastatic Breast Cancer: Final Results from a Phase II Trial
proposals.motivation: In ER+/HER2- metastatic breast cancer (MBC), the combination of the AKT inhibitor capivasertib with fulvestrant has shown promising progression-free survival (PFS) benefit, particularly in tumors with PTEN mutations or other PI3K/AKT/PTEN pathway alterations. However, as of 2025, mature overall survival (OS) data from pivotal trials are not yet fully reported. Confirming an OS benefit is critical, as it remains the definitive efficacy endpoint for regulatory approval and clinical adoption, ensuring the treatment delivers a meaningful survival advantage beyond delaying progression.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the significant improvement in PFS observed with capivasertib plus fulvestrant versus fulvestrant alone in PTEN-mutant ER+ MBC will translate into a statistically significant and clinically meaningful improvement in overall survival with extended follow-up and mature data.
proposals.method: We will conduct a final, pre-specified OS analysis of a completed Phase II trial cohort of PTEN-mutant ER+ MBC patients randomized to capivasertib-fulvestrant or placebo-fulvestrant. The primary endpoint is OS, analyzed using a stratified log-rank test with a two-sided alpha of 0.05, stratified by prior CDK4/6 inhibitor use and visceral metastasis status. Secondary analyses will include landmark OS rates at 24 and 36 months, Cox proportional hazards modeling to adjust for key prognostic factors, and exploratory subgroup analyses based on baseline genomic features (e.g., co-mutations, PTEN loss by IHC) assessed from archival tumor tissue and serial cfDNA NGS data collected during the study.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide definitive evidence on the survival impact of combined AKT and ER targeting in a molecularly defined population, directly informing treatment guidelines. It will also offer a unique dataset to correlate long-term survival outcomes with baseline and on-treatment genomic markers, potentially identifying predictive biomarkers for exceptional responders or those with limited benefit.
proposals.novelty: While PFS benefits of PI3K/AKT pathway inhibitors are established, confirming OS improvement in the specific context of PTEN-mutant disease with a potent, selective AKT inhibitor like capivasertib is a crucial and unanswered question. This analysis leverages mature trial data and integrates serial cfDNA to explore dynamic predictors of long-term survival, moving beyond static baseline assessments.

proposals.title: Inferring Molecular Mechanisms of Trastuzumab Resistance from Clinical Recurrence Dynamics in HER2+ Breast Cancer
proposals.motivation: Despite trastuzumab's efficacy in HER2+ breast cancer, acquired resistance remains a major clinical challenge. Pre-2026, resistance mechanisms are often studied via pre- and post-treatment biopsies, which are invasive and may miss temporal heterogeneity. Clinical recurrence patterns—time to progression, site of relapse—represent a rich, underutilized data source that may encode the underlying molecular escape pathways driving resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct molecular mechanisms of trastuzumab resistance (e.g., PI3K/AKT activation, loss of PTEN, HER2 alterations) produce characteristic signatures in clinical recurrence data, enabling the inference of dominant resistance pathways from real-world patient trajectories.
proposals.method: We will analyze de-identified longitudinal data from patients with HER2+ metastatic breast cancer treated with trastuzumab, focusing on progression-free survival, site-specific recurrence, and serial ctDNA NGS (when available). We will develop a Bayesian inverse modeling framework where observed recurrence events are the output of a hidden Markov model representing latent molecular states (e.g., PTEN-mutant, PIK3CA-mutant). The model will be trained and validated using existing datasets with paired clinical outcomes and genomic profiling (e.g., from phase I basket studies).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, clinically applicable method to predict dominant resistance mechanisms non-invasively from routine clinical data. It will identify patient subgroups most likely to harbor specific targetable alterations (e.g., AKT activation), directly informing subsequent therapy selection, such as combining endocrine therapy or PI3K/AKT inhibitors.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Baselga et al. links specific mutations (e.g., PTEN) to drug response, this approach uniquely treats recurrence dynamics as an inverse problem to systematically back-calculate resistance mechanisms, moving beyond correlative genomic analysis.

proposals.title: Integrated Genomic and Transcriptomic Biomarkers of Everolimus Response in Hormone Receptor-Positive Metastatic Breast Cancer
proposals.motivation: Everolimus, an mTOR inhibitor, is a key therapy for HR+ metastatic breast cancer (MBC) after endocrine resistance, but only a subset of patients derive significant benefit. Current clinical use lacks predictive biomarkers, leading to unnecessary toxicity and cost for non-responders. Building on pre-2026 work showing the importance of PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway mutations (e.g., PTEN) and dynamic cfDNA analysis, there is a critical need to define a more comprehensive biomarker signature for patient selection.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a composite biomarker signature, integrating baseline tumor genomics (e.g., specific mTOR pathway mutations) with on-treatment changes in circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) and a tumor-specific gene expression network state, will accurately predict clinical benefit from everolimus plus endocrine therapy in HR+ MBC.
proposals.method: We will analyze pre-treatment and serial plasma samples from a cohort of HR+ MBC patients treated with everolimus + exemestane. Using targeted NGS panels (similar to Baselga et al.'s cfDNA studies), we will identify baseline mutations and track ctDNA dynamics. Pre-treatment tumor RNA-seq data will be used to construct patient-specific network models of the PI3K/mTOR/ER signaling axis, employing algorithms like ARACNe and VIPER to infer master regulator activity. Predictive modeling will integrate these multi-omic features using regularized regression (LASSO) and random forest classifiers to generate a response signature.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a clinically actionable biomarker model to guide everolimus use, moving beyond single-gene markers. It will advance the understanding of dynamic resistance mechanisms by linking ctDNA clearance to network-defined tumor states. The findings could directly inform the design of a prospective biomarker-stratified clinical trial.
proposals.novelty: While PTEN loss is known to confer sensitivity, this approach uniquely integrates dynamic ctDNA monitoring with patient-specific network modeling of signaling pathways to capture both genomic and functional transcriptional determinants of response, a strategy not yet applied to everolimus in the pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A Bayesian Adaptive Platform Trial to Optimize mTOR Inhibitor Combinations in PTEN-mutant, ER+ Metastatic Breast Cancer
proposals.motivation: In ER+ metastatic breast cancer (MBC), PTEN loss confers resistance to endocrine therapy and is associated with poor outcomes. Preclinical and early clinical data suggest mTOR inhibitors (e.g., everolimus) can overcome this resistance, but optimal combination partners (e.g., with fulvestrant, CDK4/6 inhibitors, or PI3K/AKT inhibitors) and predictive biomarkers remain undefined. Conventional sequential trials are too slow and inefficient to address these questions, delaying personalized treatment strategies for this high-risk population.
proposals.hypothesis: A Bayesian adaptive platform trial, incorporating serial circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) analysis, will efficiently identify the most effective mTOR inhibitor-based combination for PTEN-mutant ER+ MBC and dynamically uncover genomic and ctDNA-based biomarkers of response and resistance.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-arm, multi-stage platform trial for PTEN-mutant ER+ MBC. Patients will be randomized to an mTOR inhibitor (everolimus) combined with either fulvestrant, a CDK4/6 inhibitor, or a PI3Kα inhibitor (alpelisib). A Bayesian adaptive design using a hierarchical model will allocate patients preferentially to better-performing arms based on a primary endpoint of progression-free survival. Serial ctDNA analysis (using NGS panels covering PIK3CA, ESR1, PTEN, and mTOR pathway genes) will be performed at baseline and progression. A pre-specified Bayesian longitudinal model will correlate ctDNA dynamics with clinical response, and cell-line derived network models will be used to interpret emergent resistance mutations.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a clinically actionable, optimized combination regimen for PTEN-mutant ER+ MBC. It will establish a framework for using real-time ctDNA analysis within an adaptive trial to identify predictive biomarkers and resistance mechanisms, accelerating the development of biomarker-driven therapies. The trial design itself will serve as a model for efficiently testing multiple rational combinations in a molecularly defined population.
proposals.novelty: While basket trials test one drug across histologies, this platform adaptively tests multiple *combinations* within a single molecular subtype (PTEN-mutant ER+ MBC), integrating real-time biomarker feedback from ctDNA to guide adaptations—a significant evolution beyond static genomic basket studies.

proposals.title: Integrated Immune and Genomic Profiling to Decipher the Immunomodulatory Role of mTOR Inhibition in PTEN-mutant ER+ Metastatic Breast Cancer
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, mTOR inhibitors like everolimus combined with endocrine therapy are standard for advanced ER+ breast cancer, but response is heterogeneous. While genomic drivers like PTEN loss and PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway mutations are known, their impact on the tumor immune microenvironment (TIME) and how this influences therapeutic efficacy remains poorly characterized. Applying advanced immune profiling methods from checkpoint inhibitor trials to mTOR-targeted regimens could reveal critical immunomodulatory mechanisms of response and resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that mTOR inhibition with everolimus remodels the TIME in PTEN-mutant ER+ metastatic breast cancer, and that specific immune signatures, detectable in pre-treatment and on-treatment biopsies and cfDNA, are predictive of clinical benefit to everolimus and fulvestrant.
proposals.method: We will perform high-dimensional immune profiling on serial tumor biopsies and matched plasma from a cohort of PTEN-mutant ER+ MBC patients treated with fulvestrant and everolimus (building on prior capivasertib+fulvestrant study designs). Methods will include multiplex immunofluorescence (e.g., CODEX or Phenocycler-Fusion) to spatially map immune cell subsets (CD8+ T cells, Tregs, macrophages) and their activation states (PD-1, Ki-67) relative to tumor and PTEN-loss regions. This will be integrated with serial cfDNA NGS for dynamic genomic analysis and bulk RNA-seq for deconvolution-based immune cell quantification (using CIBERSORTx). Correlative analyses will link immune and genomic features to clinical response.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the immunomodulatory effects of mTOR inhibition in a genomically defined breast cancer subset, moving beyond cell-autonomous mechanisms. It will identify candidate immune biomarkers predictive of response to everolimus-based therapy and may reveal rational immunotherapy combinations (e.g., with PD-1 inhibition) for PTEN-mutant ER+ breast cancer.
proposals.novelty: While immune profiling is standard in immunotherapy trials, its systematic application to understand the mechanism of action of targeted therapies like mTOR inhibitors in ER+ breast cancer is novel. The integration of spatial proteomics, cfDNA, and clinical response data specifically in PTEN-mutant tumors represents a precision oncology approach to dissect treatment-induced immune changes.

proposals.title: Quantifying In Vivo Feedback Dynamics of the PI3K Pathway in ER+ Metastatic Breast Cancer Patients Treated with RAD001 (Everolimus)
proposals.motivation: Preclinical models show that mTOR inhibition by RAD001 can trigger compensatory IRS-1-driven PI3K-Akt reactivation, a key resistance mechanism. However, the magnitude and kinetics of this feedback in actual patient tumors during therapy remain poorly quantified, limiting our ability to rationally sequence or combine targeted agents. This gap is critical in PTEN-mutant and other PI3K-pathway-altered ER+ breast cancers, where combinations like fulvestrant and mTOR/PI3K/AKT inhibitors are used.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that serial patient biopsies will reveal significant, heterogeneous induction of IRS-1 and phospho-Akt (Ser473) following RAD001 therapy, and that the degree of this in vivo feedback activation correlates with early measures of treatment resistance.
proposals.method: We will perform image-based quantitative immunohistochemistry (IHC) for IRS-1 and p-Akt (S473) on serial tumor biopsies (pre-treatment and at 2-4 weeks) from a cohort of ER+ metastatic breast cancer patients treated with RAD001 +/- endocrine therapy. Digital image analysis using HALO or Aperio algorithms will quantify protein expression and subcellular localization. Dynamics will be correlated with baseline PTEN status (by IHC/sequencing) and with concurrent longitudinal circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) analysis for emerging PI3K pathway mutations, building on our prior ctDNA NGS methodologies.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative, patient-derived map of PI3K pathway feedback dynamics during mTOR inhibition, directly linking in vivo pharmacodynamic responses to genomic features. It will establish a framework for using serial biopsies and ctDNA to monitor adaptive resistance, informing the optimal timing for adding PI3K or AKT inhibitors (e.g., capivasertib) in future combination trials.
proposals.novelty: While feedback is well-studied in cell lines, this work directly measures and quantifies its dynamics in vivo in patient tumors, integrating spatial protein signaling data with temporal genomic ctDNA data to create a composite biomarker of adaptive resistance.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Profiling of PI3K/AKT Feedback Reactivation Heterogeneity in PTEN-mutant ER+ Breast Cancer Models Treated with Fulvestrant and Capivasertib
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 clinical studies, including those by Baselga et al., show that combining the AKT inhibitor capivasertib with fulvestrant improves outcomes in PTEN-mutant ER+ metastatic breast cancer, but resistance invariably emerges. Bulk tumor analyses suggest feedback reactivation of the PI3K/AKT pathway as a key resistance mechanism, but they mask critical cell-to-cell variability within the tumor ecosystem. Understanding this heterogeneity is crucial for predicting and overcoming therapeutic failure.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that within a PTEN-mutant, ER+ breast tumor treated with fulvestrant and capivasertib, a subset of cancer cells exhibits enhanced, feedback-driven Akt reactivation at the single-cell level, driven by heterogeneous transcriptional and signaling network states that are not discernible in bulk analyses.
proposals.method: We will employ single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq, 10x Genomics platform) on isogenic PTEN-mutant ER+ breast cancer cell lines (e.g., MCF7) and patient-derived organoids treated with vehicle, fulvestrant, capivasertib, or the combination. Computational analysis will use Seurat for clustering and differential expression, and SCENIC for inference of transcription factor regulon activity. We will specifically quantify heterogeneity in feedback-related signals (e.g., IRS1, IGF1R, ERBB3 expression) and correlate this with predicted PI3K/AKT pathway activity scores (using PROGENy or similar pathway inference methods) at single-cell resolution.
proposals.contribution: This work will map the landscape of single-cell feedback heterogeneity in a clinically relevant treatment context, identifying distinct cellular subpopulations primed for resistance. It will generate testable hypotheses about key transcriptional regulators driving this heterogeneity. The findings will inform the development of more effective combination therapies and biomarkers for patient stratification.
proposals.novelty: While bulk studies have implicated feedback in resistance, this proposal uniquely applies single-cell multi-omics and network analysis to deconvolute the heterogeneity of this process in PTEN-mutant models undergoing combined endocrine and AKT inhibition, moving beyond averaged signals to identify rare, resilient cell states.
