proposals.title: A Phase II Biomarker-Driven Study of Trastuzumab and Pertuzumab Without Chemotherapy in HER2-Positive Metastatic Breast Cancer Patients with Favorable Genomic Profiles
proposals.motivation: The standard first-line therapy for HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer combines dual HER2 blockade (trastuzumab + pertuzumab) with docetaxel chemotherapy, which carries significant toxicity. Pre-2026 evidence suggests a subset of patients, particularly those with specific molecular features like PIK3CA mutations, may derive substantial benefit from targeted therapy alone. De-escalating chemotherapy could preserve efficacy while markedly improving quality of life, aligning with the precision oncology paradigm.
proposals.hypothesis: Patients with HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer and a favorable genomic profile, defined by the absence of concurrent PTEN loss and specific co-mutation signatures, will achieve a comparable clinical benefit rate (CBR) at 6 months with trastuzumab and pertuzumab alone, compared to historical controls treated with the standard chemo-containing regimen.
proposals.method: We will conduct a single-arm, multicenter Phase II trial. Patients will receive trastuzumab and pertuzumab without chemotherapy. Tumor tissue will be analyzed at baseline using a targeted NGS panel (e.g., MSK-IMPACT) to define genomic profiles. Serial cfDNA will be collected at baseline, cycle 2, and progression for prospective genomic analysis using the same panel, tracking clonal evolution and emergent resistance mutations. The primary endpoint is CBR at 6 months. We will employ a Bayesian adaptive design and use network modeling of patient-derived molecular data to identify predictive signatures of response and resistance, similar to pre-2026 ER+ breast cancer studies.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first prospective evidence for a chemotherapy-free, biomarker-selected regimen in HER2-positive MBC. It will define a genomic signature predictive of excellent outcomes with dual HER2 blockade alone and identify early cfDNA-based markers of treatment failure, enabling rapid therapy adaptation. The results could establish a new, less toxic standard of care for a defined patient subgroup.
proposals.novelty: While de-escalation is being explored in early-stage HER2+ disease, this prospectively biomarker-stratified approach in the metastatic setting is novel. It directly integrates serial cfDNA monitoring and network-based resistance modeling—methods used in Dr. Baselga's recent PTEN-mutant and PI3K inhibitor studies—to dynamically understand and predict outcomes in a chemotherapy-sparing context.

proposals.title: A Network Meta-Analysis of HER2-Targeted Combination Regimens for Metastatic Breast Cancer: Informing Treatment Sequencing and Biomarker-Driven Selection
proposals.motivation: The treatment landscape for HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer (MBC) has expanded rapidly with multiple antibody-drug conjugates (ADCs), tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs), and monoclonal antibodies, often combined with chemotherapy or endocrine therapy. Direct head-to-head comparisons for all relevant regimens are lacking, creating significant uncertainty for clinicians regarding optimal first-line and later-line sequencing. A systematic, quantitative synthesis of all available evidence is urgently needed to guide clinical decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a network meta-analysis (NMA) will reveal a hierarchy of efficacy and safety for HER2-targeted combinations, identifying regimens with the most favorable benefit-risk profile for specific clinical contexts, such as prior T-DM1 exposure or presence of brain metastases.
proposals.method: We will conduct a systematic literature review to identify all phase II/III randomized controlled trials (pre-2026) evaluating HER2-targeted combinations in HER2+ MBC. Using Bayesian network meta-analysis methods (e.g., Markov Chain Monte Carlo simulation with non-informative priors in WinBUGS or R `gemtc` package), we will synthesize progression-free survival (PFS) and overall survival (OS) hazard ratios and objective response rate (ORR) data. We will perform node-splitting to assess inconsistency and rank treatments using surface under the cumulative ranking curve (SUCRA) probabilities. Subgroup analyses will be conducted where data permit for PTEN/PIK3CA mutation status, leveraging Dr. Baselga's expertise in biomarker-driven therapy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, quantitative comparison of all HER2-targeted regimens, generating clinically actionable rankings for frontline and subsequent therapies. It will highlight key evidence gaps to guide future trial design and propose hypotheses on how specific genomic alterations (e.g., PTEN loss) may modulate relative regimen efficacy, connecting clinical outcomes to mechanistic insights from Dr. Baselga's prior work on PI3K/AKT pathway resistance.
proposals.novelty: While NMAs are established in cardiology, their application in the complex, rapidly evolving HER2+ MBC space is novel. This analysis moves beyond pairwise comparisons to model the entire treatment network, incorporating biomarker subgroups—an approach not yet systematically applied to this disease setting.

proposals.title: A Machine Learning Classifier Integrating Genomic and Clinical Data to Predict Response to Pertuzumab in HER2-Positive Metastatic Breast Cancer
proposals.motivation: Despite the efficacy of pertuzumab combined with trastuzumab and docetaxel in HER2+ metastatic breast cancer, a significant proportion of patients exhibit primary or acquired resistance. Current clinical biomarkers, such as HER2 amplification, are insufficient for precise response prediction, leading to unnecessary toxicity and cost for non-responders. Pre-2026 advances in genomic profiling and machine learning offer a timely opportunity to develop more accurate, individualized predictive models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a machine learning classifier trained on integrated genomic data (e.g., mutations, copy number variations, gene expression) and baseline clinical variables can accurately predict objective response to pertuzumab-based first-line therapy in HER2+ metastatic breast cancer.
proposals.method: We will leverage a curated, multi-institutional cohort of HER2+ MBC patients treated with pertuzumab/trastuzumab/docetaxel with pre-treatment tumor sequencing (whole exome or targeted NGS panels) and annotated clinical outcomes (RECIST response). Feature engineering will include mutation status of key pathway genes (e.g., PIK3CA, PTEN, TP53), genomic signatures, and clinical variables. We will train and compare multiple supervised classifiers, including regularized logistic regression (LASSO), random forests, and gradient boosting machines (XGBoost), using nested cross-validation to optimize hyperparameters and prevent overfitting. Model performance will be assessed by AUC-ROC, precision-recall, and clinical utility metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, interpretable predictive model for pertuzumab response, potentially identifying novel genomic correlates of resistance. It will provide a methodological framework for integrating complex genomic data into clinical prediction in oncology. The findings could guide future biomarker-stratified clinical trials and inform more personalized treatment selection.
proposals.novelty: While machine learning has been applied to predict response to therapies like fulvestrant in ER+ disease, its focused application to pertuzumab in HER2+ breast cancer, specifically integrating pre-treatment mutational data with clinical features, is novel. The approach builds directly on Dr. Baselga's expertise in PI3K/AKT pathway biology and resistance but applies it to a different therapeutic axis.

proposals.title: A Phase II Study of Response-Adapted De-escalation: Dropping Chemotherapy After Initial Response to Trastuzumab and Pertuzumab in HER2-Positive Metastatic Breast Cancer
proposals.motivation: The standard first-line therapy for HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer combines dual HER2 blockade (trastuzumab + pertuzumab) with chemotherapy (docetaxel), which achieves high response rates but exposes patients to significant cumulative toxicity. Pre-2026, treatment de-escalation strategies are underexplored in metastatic disease, despite their success in early-stage HER2+ breast cancer. This study addresses the critical need to reduce treatment burden while maintaining efficacy for patients who achieve a deep initial response.
proposals.hypothesis: In patients with HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer who achieve a confirmed objective response or stable disease after 4-6 cycles of induction therapy with trastuzumab, pertuzumab, and docetaxel, de-escalation to trastuzumab and pertuzumab maintenance alone will result in a 12-month progression-free survival rate non-inferior to historical controls on continuous chemotherapy.
proposals.method: We propose a single-arm, multicenter, phase II trial. Patients receive induction with trastuzumab, pertuzumab, and docetaxel. Those with a confirmed response (RECIST 1.1) after 4-6 cycles de-escalate to dual HER2 blockade alone. The primary endpoint is 12-month PFS rate. Serial cfDNA analysis (using a 73-gene NGS panel) will be performed at baseline, at de-escalation, and at progression to identify genomic predictors of successful de-escalation (e.g., clearance of HER2-amplified cfDNA) and mechanisms of resistance (e.g., emergent *PTEN* mutations, *PIK3CA* mutations, or *ESR1* mutations). Tumor biopsies at progression will be analyzed via RNA-seq to construct patient-specific network models of resistance, as previously described in ER+ breast cancer studies.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first prospective evidence for a chemotherapy-free maintenance strategy in metastatic HER2+ breast cancer, directly reducing patient toxicity. It will generate a genomic dataset linking cfDNA dynamics to clinical outcomes after de-escalation, identifying biomarkers for patient selection. The resistance network models will nominate rational drug combinations (e.g., adding a PI3Kα or AKT inhibitor) for patients who progress on maintenance therapy.
proposals.novelty: While de-escalation is established in early breast cancer, applying this paradigm to metastatic disease is a novel and counter-intuitive approach. The integration of serial cfDNA monitoring and network modeling to guide and understand de-escalation moves beyond empirical clinical observation to a mechanism-driven strategy.

proposals.title: Integrated Genomic and Proteomic Analysis of PTEN/PI3K Pathway Activity and HER2 Dynamics as Predictors of T-DM1 Response in HER2-Positive Metastatic Breast Cancer
proposals.motivation: Despite T-DM1's efficacy in HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer, a significant proportion of patients exhibit primary or acquired resistance, leading to disease progression. Current clinical decision-making relies primarily on HER2 status, which is insufficient to predict heterogeneous treatment outcomes. Identifying robust molecular biomarkers is critical to personalize therapy and improve patient survival, mirroring the precision medicine approaches successfully applied to other targeted therapies like fulvestrant combinations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that intrinsic resistance to T-DM1 is driven by specific co-alterations, particularly in the PI3K/PTEN pathway and in regulators of HER2 membrane trafficking and recycling, which modulate antibody-drug conjugate internalization and payload efficacy.
proposals.method: We will leverage pre-treatment tumor biopsies and serial cfDNA from a well-annotated cohort of ER+/HER2+ MBC patients treated with T-DM2. Using targeted NGS (similar to the 'Utility of Serial cfDNA' study), we will profile mutations in PIK3CA, PTEN, and other pathway genes. We will integrate this with reverse-phase protein array (RPPA) data to quantify phospho-protein levels of AKT, S6, and HER2. Predictive modeling will be performed using logistic regression and random forest algorithms to identify biomarker signatures associated with objective response and progression-free survival, validated in independent cell line datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will define a composite biomarker signature that refines patient selection for T-DM1 beyond HER2 status. It will provide mechanistic insight into resistance by linking genomic alterations to functional protein pathway activity and HER2 biology. The findings could directly inform the design of rational combination therapies, such as adding PI3K/AKT inhibitors (e.g., capivasertib) to T-DM1, to overcome resistance.
proposals.novelty: While PI3K pathway alterations are known resistance factors for HER2-targeted tyrosine kinase inhibitors, their specific role in modulating the efficacy of the ADC T-DM1, which requires internalization, is less defined. This proposal uniquely integrates cfDNA genomics with dynamic proteomic pathway activation and applies network modeling principles (as in Dr. Baselga's cell line studies) to a clinical ADC response dataset.

proposals.title: Integrating Continuous Residual Cancer Burden with Genomic Profiling to Refine Prognostication and Predict Resistance in ER+/HER2- Breast Cancer Treated with Endocrine-Targeted Combinations
proposals.motivation: Pathological complete response (pCR) is a binary endpoint that fails to capture the spectrum of residual disease after neoadjuvant therapy, discarding prognostically significant information. In ER+ breast cancer, where pCR rates are low, a more granular measure of response is critical for evaluating novel endocrine-based combinations, such as fulvestrant with PI3K/AKT inhibitors. Pre-2026 work by Baselga et al. highlights the importance of mutations (e.g., PTEN) and longitudinal cfDNA analysis in these contexts, but these genomic features have not been systematically integrated with continuous pathological response metrics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that continuous Residual Cancer Burden (RCB) scores, when integrated with baseline and on-treatment genomic data (e.g., PTEN/PIK3CA mutations, cfDNA dynamics), will provide a superior, multidimensional predictor of long-term outcomes and will identify distinct biological mechanisms of residual disease following treatment with fulvestrant and PI3K/AKT pathway inhibitors.
proposals.method: We will analyze a cohort of ER+/HER2- breast cancer patients from prior neoadjuvant/advanced trials (e.g., involving fulvestrant + capivasertib) with available RCB scores from central pathology review. Using pre-2026 validated methods, we will perform correlative analysis between RCB (as a continuous variable) and baseline tumor mutations (via NGS) and serial cfDNA changes (modeled as mutant allele frequency kinetics). We will employ multivariate Cox regression and machine learning algorithms (specifically, regularized regression like LASSO and Random Forest) to build a composite model predicting distant relapse-free survival, comparing its performance to binary pCR.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a more nuanced framework for assessing efficacy in ER+ breast cancer clinical trials, moving beyond pCR. It will generate a validated, integrated biomarker model that combines pathological and genomic data to improve patient risk stratification and identify those with residual disease despite targeted therapy. The findings will guide the design of future studies by pinpointing molecular features associated with therapeutic resistance.
proposals.novelty: While RCB is established, its integration with dynamic, trial-specific genomic data from targeted endocrine-kinase inhibitor combinations is novel. This approach directly tests whether continuous pathological response reveals stronger genomic correlates than the pCR binary, offering a new lens on resistance mechanisms in the specific context of PI3K/AKT inhibition.

proposals.title: Subtype-Dependent Prognostic Value of Pathologic Complete Response in Breast Cancer: A Genomically-Annotated Analysis of Neoadjuvant Trials
proposals.motivation: Pathologic complete response (pCR) is a key surrogate endpoint in neoadjuvant breast cancer trials, but its prognostic significance is assumed to be uniform across subtypes. This assumption may be flawed, as the biological drivers and clinical outcomes of luminal, HER2-positive, and triple-negative breast cancers (TNBC) differ profoundly. A nuanced, subtype-specific understanding of pCR's value is critical for accurate trial interpretation and regulatory decision-making in the pre-2026 era of precision oncology.
proposals.hypothesis: The association between pCR and long-term outcomes (distant recurrence-free survival, overall survival) is significantly modified by breast cancer subtype and by specific genomic alterations, such as PTEN loss or PIK3CA mutations, which influence tumor biology and therapeutic sensitivity.
proposals.method: We will perform a pooled, patient-level meta-analysis of neoadjuvant trials, integrating clinical outcomes with genomic data from tumor sequencing and serial circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) analysis. The primary analysis will use multivariate Cox proportional hazards models with interaction terms to test if the hazard ratio linking pCR to survival differs by subtype. Secondary analyses will stratify by genomic alterations (e.g., PTEN, PIK3CA, ESR1) identified from pre-treatment biopsies and ctDNA dynamics. Machine learning (regularized regression, random forests) will be used to identify composite biomarkers that predict for a 'high-value' pCR associated with maximal survival benefit.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, genomically-informed framework for interpreting pCR, moving beyond a one-size-fits-all endpoint. It will guide the design of future neoadjuvant trials by identifying patient subsets where pCR is a robust surrogate and others where alternative endpoints are needed. The findings will directly impact clinical practice by refining prognostic counseling for patients based on their subtype and genomic profile.
proposals.novelty: While pCR's prognostic strength is known to vary by subtype, this study uniquely integrates detailed genomic annotation and ctDNA dynamics to mechanistically explain this heterogeneity and to define biomarker-stratified contexts of pCR utility, building directly on Dr. Baselga's work in PTEN-mutant ER+ disease and serial cfDNA analysis.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating Pathologic Complete Response as a Surrogate Endpoint in ER+/HER2- Breast Cancer: A Comparative Analysis of Chemotherapy vs. Targeted Therapy (CDK4/6 Inhibitor) Regimens
proposals.motivation: In ER+/HER2- breast cancer, pathologic complete response (pCR) after neoadjuvant chemotherapy is a well-established but imperfect surrogate for long-term outcomes like event-free survival (EFS). The increasing use of targeted agents like CDK4/6 inhibitors (e.g., palbociclib) combined with endocrine therapy (e.g., fulvestrant) in the metastatic and, investigatively, neoadjuvant settings raises a critical question: does the pCR-EFS relationship validated in chemotherapy trials hold when the therapeutic mechanism shifts from cytotoxic to targeted cell-cycle inhibition? This is crucial for accelerating drug development and interpreting trial results for these novel regimens.
proposals.hypothesis: The association between pCR and improved long-term outcomes (EFS, OS) is significantly attenuated in patients receiving neoadjuvant CDK4/6 inhibitor plus endocrine therapy compared to those receiving traditional neoadjuvant chemotherapy, due to differing mechanisms of action and residual disease biology.
proposals.method: We will perform a pooled, patient-level meta-analysis of neoadjuvant trials in ER+/HER2- breast cancer, segregating cohorts by treatment type: 1) Chemotherapy-based, and 2) CDK4/6 inhibitor + endocrine therapy-based (e.g., from trials like NeoPalAna). Using pre-2026 statistical methods, we will model the pCR-EFS relationship within each cohort using multivariate Cox proportional hazards regression, adjusting for clinical stage and grade. The key analysis will be a formal interaction test between treatment type (chemotherapy vs. targeted) and pCR status on EFS. Serial cfDNA data (where available) will be analyzed to correlate molecular response (e.g., mutant ESR1, PIK3CA clearance) with pCR and EFS in the targeted therapy cohort.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct evidence on whether pCR is a valid surrogate endpoint in the context of modern targeted therapy for ER+ breast cancer. It will offer crucial guidance for clinical trial design and regulatory decision-making. Furthermore, by integrating cfDNA dynamics, it may identify superior, mechanism-specific biomarkers of response for CDK4/6 inhibitor regimens.
proposals.novelty: While pCR's utility is debated, no study has directly and quantitatively compared its prognostic strength across fundamentally different therapeutic modalities (cytotoxic vs. targeted) within the same cancer subtype. This analysis leverages emerging neoadjuvant targeted therapy trial data to test a foundational assumption in oncology drug development.

proposals.title: A Multi-modal, Genomically-Informed Model to Refine Pathologic Complete Response as a Surrogate Endpoint for Survival in Triple-Negative Breast Cancer
proposals.motivation: Pathologic complete response (pCR) is a strong prognostic marker in triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC), but its validity as a surrogate endpoint for accelerated drug approval is debated due to heterogeneity in patient outcomes. Pre-2026, the KEYNOTE-522 trial established pCR as a key metric for pembrolizumab in early TNBC, yet not all patients achieving pCR experience long-term survival, and some non-pCR patients do. This variability suggests that pCR's surrogacy value is modulated by underlying tumor biology, which remains poorly characterized at the individual level.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating baseline genomic features (e.g., homologous recombination deficiency signatures, PI3K/PTEN pathway status) and on-treatment circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) dynamics with pCR status will generate a composite, TNBC-specific surrogate endpoint that more accurately predicts long-term survival than pCR alone.
proposals.method: We will analyze patient-level data from completed neoadjuvant TNBC trials (pre-2026), including genomic profiling from tumor tissue (e.g., PTEN loss, PIK3CA mutations) and serial ctDNA next-generation sequencing (NGS) data. Using a machine learning framework (e.g., random survival forests or a Cox proportional hazards model with regularization), we will develop a multi-variable model where pCR is one input among several. The model will be trained to predict event-free survival, and its performance will be validated against the Prentice criteria for surrogate endpoints. Specific algorithms will include feature selection via LASSO and internal validation via bootstrapping.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a more nuanced, biologically-informed framework for using pCR in TNBC drug development, potentially accelerating the evaluation of novel therapies like AKT inhibitors (e.g., capivasertib) or PARP inhibitors in genomically defined subsets. It will offer clinical trialists a validated, composite surrogate endpoint that improves risk stratification and trial interpretation beyond the binary pCR/non-pCR dichotomy.
proposals.novelty: While pCR-survival associations are known, this proposal uniquely integrates pre-treatment mutational status and on-treatment ctDNA clearance—methods central to Dr. Baselga's recent work—to *refine* pCR as a surrogate, moving from a population-level correlation to a patient-level, mechanism-adjusted prediction model.

proposals.title: Validating Pathologic Complete Response as a Surrogate Endpoint for Long-Term Outcomes in Rare, Actionable Subtypes of Early Breast Cancer: A Single-Institution, Deeply Phenotyped Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Pathologic complete response (pCR) is an established surrogate for survival in HER2-positive and triple-negative breast cancer, but its validity in rare, molecularly defined ER+ subtypes (e.g., PTEN-mutant, AKT-altered, or specific ESR1 mutations) remains unproven. These subtypes are underrepresented in large randomized neoadjuvant trials, creating uncertainty in using pCR for accelerated drug development and clinical decision-making in these populations. A focused validation in these rare groups is critical for translating biomarker-driven therapies from the metastatic (e.g., fulvestrant + AKTi) to the early-stage setting.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that achieving pCR following neoadjuvant therapy is a statistically valid surrogate endpoint for event-free survival (EFS) in patients with rare, actionable molecular subtypes of early breast cancer, specifically those with alterations in the PI3K/AKT/PTEN pathway. This association will be stronger in these defined subtypes than in unselected ER+ disease.
proposals.method: We will apply a pooled analysis framework, as used in the CTNeoBC meta-analysis, to a single-institution, retrospectively assembled cohort of patients with early breast cancer treated with neoadjuvant therapy (2010-2025). Patients will be stratified by comprehensive genomic profiling (using institutional NGS panels, including PTEN, PIK3CA, AKT1, and ESR1) from tumor tissue or serial cfDNA. The primary analysis will correlate pCR (ypT0/is ypN0) with 3-year and 5-year EFS within each molecular subtype. Statistical validation will use the Prentice criteria and weighted linear regression of trial-level treatment effects (log-odds pCR vs. log-hazard EFS) across molecularly defined subgroups, adapting methods from previous surrogate endpoint validations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first evidence-based framework for using pCR as a surrogate endpoint in clinical trials targeting rare breast cancer subtypes, accelerating the evaluation of targeted combinations like PI3K/AKT inhibitors with endocrine therapy. It will offer clinically actionable guidance for interpreting neoadjuvant trial results and treatment response in molecularly selected patients, bridging the gap between metastatic biomarker studies and early-stage drug development.
proposals.novelty: While pooled analyses have validated pCR in broad subtypes, this study uniquely scales down the framework to institution-level, deep molecular phenotyping to power analysis in rare subgroups, directly linking pre-2026 translational research on specific mutations (e.g., PTEN) and drug resistance to surrogate endpoint validation for early-stage therapy.

proposals.title: A Pan-Cancer Meta-Analysis of Pathologic Complete Response as a Surrogate Endpoint for Overall Survival in Solid Tumors Treated with Neoadjuvant Systemic Therapy
proposals.motivation: The CTNeoBC meta-analysis established pCR as a surrogate endpoint for long-term outcomes in HER2-positive and triple-negative breast cancer, accelerating drug development. However, the generalizability of this framework to other solid tumors receiving neoadjuvant therapy (e.g., gastroesophageal, bladder, lung, sarcoma) remains unvalidated. A pan-cancer assessment is urgently needed to determine if pCR can serve as a universal, tumor-agnostic regulatory endpoint for novel targeted and immunotherapies in the neoadjuvant setting.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the strength of the association between pCR and event-free survival (EFS) or overall survival (OS) varies significantly across tumor types and molecular subtypes, and that this association can be strengthened by incorporating baseline genomic features (e.g., PTEN loss, PI3K pathway mutations) and on-treatment ctDNA dynamics.
proposals.method: We will perform a systematic literature review and individual patient data meta-analysis of randomized neoadjuvant trials across all solid tumors (pre-2026). Following the CTNeoBC statistical framework, we will quantify trial-level and patient-level associations between pCR and EFS/OS. We will integrate genomic data (e.g., from MSK-IMPACT-like panels) to assess how specific mutations (PTEN, PIK3CA, etc.) modulate the surrogacy relationship. Serial circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) data from available studies will be analyzed to evaluate if clearance enhances the predictive value of pCR for survival.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, evidence-based guideline on the use of pCR as a surrogate endpoint across solid tumors, directly informing clinical trial design and regulatory strategy. It will identify tumor contexts where pCR is a valid surrogate and others where it is not, preventing its misuse. The integration of genomic and ctDNA data will offer a more precise, biologically-informed surrogacy model.
proposals.novelty: While CTNeoBC was tumor-specific, this is the first attempt to create a pan-cancer surrogacy framework, moving beyond breast cancer. The integration of mutational profiles and ctDNA dynamics with the classic surrogacy analysis is novel and aligns with the precision oncology paradigm dominant pre-2026.

proposals.title: Inverse Modeling of Neoadjuvant Treatment Efficacy from Pathological Complete Response Patterns in ER+ Breast Cancer Subtypes
proposals.motivation: Neoadjuvant therapy response, measured by pathological complete response (pCR), is a critical biomarker for long-term outcomes in breast cancer. However, predicting which specific treatment regimen is most likely to have produced an observed pattern of subtype-specific pCR rates across a population remains an unsolved inverse problem. Solving this could identify optimal, subtype-targeted treatment strategies from real-world outcome data, moving beyond forward prediction of response.
proposals.hypothesis: By applying inverse statistical and machine learning models to curated, publicly available neoadjuvant clinical trial data, we can accurately infer the most probable administered treatment regimens (e.g., chemotherapy + targeted agent combinations) given observed pCR rate patterns across molecularly defined subgroups, such as those with PTEN mutations or PI3K/AKT pathway alterations.
proposals.method: We will aggregate pCR rates and treatment data from pre-2026 neoadjuvant trials (e.g., I-SPY 2, NeoALTTO). For each cancer subtype (defined by ER, HER2, and genomic markers like PTEN), we will model the relationship between treatment components and pCR probability using Bayesian hierarchical logistic regression. We will then frame the inverse problem as a Bayesian inference task, using Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling to compute the posterior probability distribution over possible treatment regimens given a target pCR pattern. Model validation will use held-out trial arms and simulated data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational framework to reason backwards from population-level outcomes to probable therapeutic causes, offering a data-driven tool for regimen optimization. It will generate testable hypotheses about the most effective treatment combinations for specific genomic subsets of ER+ breast cancer, directly informing the design of future biomarker-driven neoadjuvant studies.
proposals.novelty: While forward models predict pCR from treatment and biomarkers, inverting this relationship to infer treatment from pCR patterns is a novel, clinically relevant approach. It uniquely leverages Dr. Baselga's expertise in PTEN-mutant breast cancer and targeted therapies like PI3K/AKT inhibitors to ground the inverse model in biologically plausible mechanisms.

proposals.title: A Pragmatic, Adaptive Phase III Trial to Validate the Clinical Utility and Cost-Effectiveness of Pharmacokinetic-Guided Trastuzumab Dosing in HER2-Positive Metastatic Breast Cancer
proposals.motivation: Trastuzumab is a cornerstone of HER2-positive breast cancer therapy, but standard weight-based dosing leads to highly variable drug exposure, potentially compromising efficacy or increasing toxicity and cost. Pre-2026 studies, including population PK models and retrospective analyses, suggest that maintaining trough concentrations (Ctrough) above a threshold (e.g., ~20 µg/mL) improves outcomes, but this has not been prospectively validated in a definitive, pragmatic trial setting. Implementing a PK-guided strategy without proof of a net clinical and economic benefit would add complexity and cost to patient care unjustifiably.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that PK-guided dosing of trastuzumab, targeting a Ctrough ≥20 µg/mL, will improve progression-free survival (PFS) compared to standard fixed-weight-based dosing, and that this improvement will be cost-effective within accepted healthcare thresholds.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, randomized, open-label, adaptive phase III trial. Patients with HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer starting first-line trastuzumab + pertuzumab + taxane will be randomized 1:1 to standard dosing or PK-guided dosing. In the intervention arm, a validated Bayesian forecasting algorithm (using NONMEM-based population PK models) will utilize sparse PK sampling (at cycles 1, 3, and 6) to recommend individualized dose adjustments to achieve the target Ctrough. The primary endpoint is investigator-assessed PFS. An adaptive design includes a pre-planned futility analysis at 50% of PFS events. A concurrent, prospective health economic evaluation will collect resource use and EQ-5D-5L data to calculate incremental cost per quality-adjusted life-year (QALY) gained from a payer perspective.
proposals.contribution: This trial will provide the first high-level evidence from a pragmatic, randomized design on whether PK-guided dosing improves a hard efficacy endpoint in HER2+ metastatic breast cancer. It will deliver a definitive answer on clinical utility, preventing the adoption of a complex intervention without proven benefit. Concurrently, it will generate robust, prospective data on cost-effectiveness, crucial for guiding reimbursement and clinical practice decisions globally.
proposals.novelty: While therapeutic drug monitoring is common in other fields, its application in oncology biologics like trastuzumab lacks phase III validation. This study's novelty lies in its integrated, pragmatic, and adversarial design—explicitly testing for superiority in a real-world efficacy endpoint while concurrently evaluating cost-effectiveness, with a built-in futility rule to halt the concept if it fails to demonstrate clear added value early.

proposals.title: Prospective Evaluation of Pharmacokinetic-Guided Dosing of Trastuzumab in HER2-Positive Metastatic Breast Cancer: A Randomized Phase II Study
proposals.motivation: Trastuzumab, a cornerstone of HER2-positive breast cancer therapy, is administered via fixed weight-based dosing, which ignores significant inter-patient variability in drug clearance and exposure. Pre-2026 evidence suggests that suboptimal drug exposure may compromise efficacy or increase toxicity. This study addresses a critical unmet need by testing a precision dosing strategy to optimize trastuzumab therapy, aligning with Dr. Baselga's expertise in targeted therapy and biomarker-driven trials.
proposals.hypothesis: Individualized, pharmacokinetic (PK)-guided dosing of trastuzumab, targeting a pre-defined trough concentration (Ctrough), will improve the clinical benefit rate (CBR) and reduce the incidence of symptomatic cardiac toxicity compared to standard fixed dosing in patients with HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer.
proposals.method: We will conduct a randomized, open-label, phase II study. Patients will be stratified by prior trastuzumab exposure and randomized 1:1 to standard dosing (8 mg/kg load, then 6 mg/kg q3w) or PK-guided dosing (initial standard load, then dose adjustments at Cycle 3 to achieve a target Ctrough of ≥20 µg/mL, based on a validated population PK model and Bayesian forecasting). The primary endpoint is CBR at 24 weeks. Serial plasma sampling for PK analysis and cfDNA for genomic evolution (using NGS panels tracking HER2 and relevant resistance pathways like PI3K/PTEN) will be performed. Statistical analysis will use a two-sided chi-square test for the primary endpoint.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first prospective, randomized evidence for PK-guided dosing of a monoclonal antibody in breast cancer. It will establish a framework for precision dosing of biologics and generate correlative data on how drug exposure influences genomic evolution of tumors and the emergence of resistance mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: While therapeutic drug monitoring is established for small molecules, its application to monoclonal antibodies like trastuzumab in oncology is novel. This study integrates real-time PK adaptation with serial cfDNA analysis, creating a dynamic, closed-loop feedback system for treatment personalization beyond static genomic biomarkers.

proposals.title: A Multi-Omic Machine Learning Framework for Pre-Treatment Risk Stratification of Trastuzumab-Anthracycline Cardiotoxicity in HER2+ Breast Cancer
proposals.motivation: Trastuzumab plus anthracycline-based chemotherapy is a cornerstone for HER2+ breast cancer, but its use is limited by a significant risk of cardiotoxicity, including heart failure. Current risk assessment is based on clinical factors like age and baseline LVEF, which lack precision, leading to reactive monitoring and treatment modifications after damage may have occurred. A pre-emptive, biology-driven stratification tool is urgently needed to personalize treatment selection and cardioprotection from the outset.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating baseline genomic (e.g., germline polymorphisms in ERBB2, TOP2A, RARG), proteomic (e.g., serum HER2, troponin), and metabolomic profiles with clinical data will enable the development of a robust machine learning model that can accurately predict severe cardiotoxicity risk before treatment initiation.
proposals.method: We will train and validate models on a deeply phenotyped cohort of HER2+ breast cancer patients treated with trastuzumab-anthracycline, with serial cardiac imaging and biomarker data. The multi-omic data will be integrated using an ensemble model architecture, combining a random forest for feature selection and interpretability with a gradient boosting machine (e.g., XGBoost) for final prediction. Model performance will be assessed via cross-validation and on a held-out test set, with a focus on sensitivity for high-risk identification.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, clinically-actionable risk score to guide initial regimen choice, enabling clinicians to consider alternative sequences (e.g., non-anthracycline backbones) or targeted cardioprotection for high-risk patients. It will establish a framework for precision cardio-oncology by moving from reactive monitoring to pre-emptive, biology-informed decision-making.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have examined single biomarkers, this approach integrates multi-omic data at baseline specifically for pre-treatment prediction, aligning with Dr. Baselga's expertise in PI3K/AKT pathway biology and resistance mechanisms, which are implicated in both cancer progression and cardiac cell survival.

proposals.title: Somatic Copy Number Alterations in Breast Tumors as Genomic Predictors of Trastuzumab-Anthracycline Cardiotoxicity
proposals.motivation: Trastuzumab and anthracyclines are cornerstone therapies for HER2-positive breast cancer, but their combined use is limited by a significant risk of cardiotoxicity, including left ventricular dysfunction. Current risk stratification relies on clinical factors and lacks tumor-intrinsic genomic predictors. This project addresses a critical unmet need to identify patients at highest risk, enabling personalized cardiac surveillance and intervention to improve treatment safety.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, previously uncharacterized somatic copy number alterations (SCNAs) in breast tumors modulate cardiac cell susceptibility to trastuzumab and anthracycline toxicity, and that these SCNAs can serve as predictive biomarkers for clinical cardiotoxicity.
proposals.method: We will integrate detailed clinical cardiotoxicity data (e.g., serial LVEF measurements) from a retrospective cohort of HER2+ breast cancer patients treated with trastuzumab-anthracycline regimens with high-resolution genomic profiling (using SNP arrays or whole-exome sequencing) of their primary tumors. An unbiased association analysis will be performed using GISTIC 2.0 to identify focal SCNAs within 122 predefined unexplained regions correlated with cardiotoxicity endpoints. Validated SCNA loci will be functionally interrogated in human induced pluripotent stem cell-derived cardiomyocytes (iPSC-CMs) co-cultured with breast cancer cell lines harboring engineered SCNAs, assessing markers of cardiomyocyte survival, oxidative stress, and HER2 signaling pathway activity.
proposals.contribution: This work will define a novel class of tumor-acquired, predictive genomic biomarkers for a major clinical toxicity. It will provide a mechanistic link between the tumor genome and off-target cardiac effects, advancing the field of cardio-oncology. The findings could directly inform the development of a genomic companion diagnostic to guide cardiac monitoring protocols.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has focused on host germline genetics, this proposal uniquely posits that the somatic tumor genome itself directly influences susceptibility to drug-induced cardiotoxicity. It moves beyond known oncogenic drivers to mine unexplored, non-coding or passenger SCNAs for their systemic biological effects.

proposals.title: A Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis Framework to Identify the Pareto-Optimal Chemotherapy Backbone for Trastuzumab in HER2+ Metastatic Breast Cancer
proposals.motivation: The selection of a chemotherapy backbone for trastuzumab in HER2+ metastatic breast cancer (MBC) remains a critical, unresolved clinical question. Current practice relies on sequential pairwise trial comparisons, which fail to simultaneously weigh the complex trade-offs between efficacy (PFS, OS) and tolerability (toxicity, QoL). A patient-centric, data-driven framework is needed to optimize this backbone selection beyond simple efficacy rankings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying a multi-criteria optimization framework, analogous to high-throughput materials screening, to a multi-arm clinical trial will identify one or more chemotherapy backbones that lie on the Pareto-optimal frontier, offering the best composite balance of efficacy and tolerability for patients.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-arm, multi-stage (MAMS) phase II/III trial concurrently testing trastuzumab with docetaxel, carboplatin/paclitaxel, and vinorelbine. The primary endpoint will be a composite utility score derived from pre-specified weights for PFS, OS, CTCAE-graded toxicities, and patient-reported QoL (EORTC QLQ-C30). We will employ Bayesian response-adaptive randomization to favor better-performing arms and use multi-criteria decision analysis (MCDA) with a TOPSIS algorithm to rank regimens and identify the Pareto-optimal set.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, quantitative ranking of trastuzumab-chemotherapy regimens based on a holistic patient-centric endpoint. It will establish a novel clinical trial and analysis framework for therapeutic backbone optimization that can be adapted to other combination therapies in oncology.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in its direct adaptation of combinatorial optimization principles from materials science to a live clinical trial design and its explicit, quantitative integration of tolerability and QoL with traditional efficacy metrics to define an optimal regimen.

proposals.title: A HER2-Targeted Antibody-Protein Toxin Conjugate with a Tumor-Microenvironment Responsive Linker for the Treatment of HER2-Positive Breast Cancer
proposals.motivation: Despite the success of antibody-drug conjugates (ADCs) like T-DM1, their efficacy can be limited by payload potency, resistance mechanisms, and systemic toxicity from non-specific linker cleavage. The development of more potent and selectively activated cytotoxic agents is a critical unmet need in HER2-positive breast cancer, particularly for patients with mutations in pathways like PI3K/PTEN that may confer resistance to targeted therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: Conjugating a highly potent, engineered protein toxin (e.g., a human ribonuclease variant or pro-apoptotic peptide) to an anti-HER2 antibody via a linker specifically cleaved by tumor-microenvironment proteases (e.g., MMP-9) will yield a therapeutic agent with superior tumor-specific cytotoxicity and a wider therapeutic index compared to standard chemotherapy-based ADCs, especially in models with PTEN loss or PI3K pathway activation.
proposals.method: We will engineer a humanized anti-HER2 antibody (trastuzumab biosimilar) linked to a engineered, hyper-active variant of human pancreatic ribonuclease (HP-RNase) or a BH3-mimetic peptide via a MMP-9-cleavable peptide linker. Conjugate stability, binding affinity, and selective protease activation will be validated in vitro. Efficacy will be tested against a panel of ER+/HER2+ and HER2+ breast cancer cell lines, including isogenic pairs with and without PTEN mutations, using cell viability and apoptosis assays. In vivo antitumor activity and toxicity will be evaluated in patient-derived xenograft (PDX) models with serial cfDNA analysis to monitor genomic evolution and response.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel class of biologics, antibody-protein toxin conjugates (APTCs), for oncology. It will provide proof-of-concept for using highly potent, human-derived protein toxins and microenvironment-specific activation to overcome ADC limitations. The study will also generate crucial data on the efficacy of this approach in PTEN-mutant, therapy-resistant contexts, informing future clinical development.
proposals.novelty: This approach is distinct from standard ADCs by employing a potent human protein toxin instead of a small-molecule chemotherapeutic, and by utilizing a tumor-microenvironment cleavable linker for enhanced selectivity, a strategy inspired by biological pest control but novel in targeted cancer therapy.

ideas.description: This research program investigates the hypothesis that somatic copy number alterations (SCNAs) within the 122 unexplained focal regions in breast tumors harbor genes that influence cardiac cell susceptibility to trastuzumab and anthracycline toxicity. By integrating cardiac toxicity clinical data from patients treated with these regimens with detailed genomic profiling of their tumors, we will perform an unbiased screen to identify SCNAs correlated with cardiotoxicity. Validated hits will be mechanistically studied in cardio-oncology model systems to determine if they modulate pathways like HER2 signaling, oxidative stress response, or cardiomyocyte survival. The goal is to define a novel class of tumor-acquired, predictive genomic biomarkers that enable pre-emptive cardiac monitoring and mitigation strategies tailored to an individual's tumor genome, thereby improving the safety of combination therapies.

ideas.title: Pragmatic trial to validate the clinical utility and cost-effectiveness of pharmacokinetic-guided trastuzumab dosing
ideas.description: This research initiates a large-scale, multi-center randomized controlled trial with an adaptive design to first confirm or refute the clinical value of pharmacokinetic (PK)-guided trastuzumab dosing. Patients are randomized to receive either standard weight-based dosing or PK-guided dosing targeting a pre-specified exposure threshold, with the primary endpoint being a clinically meaningful efficacy outcome. The trial incorporates a pre-planned futility analysis and a concurrent prospective health economic evaluation to assess cost per quality-adjusted life-year gained. This design ensures the concept advances only if it demonstrates clear, actionable superiority over the current standard, preventing the implementation of a complex, costly dosing strategy without proven net benefit.

ideas.title: A Multi-Criteria Optimization Framework for Trastuzumab Chemotherapy Backbone Selection
ideas.description: This research adapts a combinatorial optimization framework from materials science to systematically identify the optimal chemotherapy partner for trastuzumab. In materials science, high-throughput screening of alloy compositions against multiple performance criteria is used to find Pareto-optimal solutions. The adapted idea proposes a multi-arm, multi-stage clinical trial that concurrently tests various chemotherapy backbones (e.g., docetaxel, carboplatin/paclitaxel, vinorelbine) combined with trastuzumab. Each regimen is evaluated on a composite, weighted endpoint balancing efficacy (progression-free survival, overall survival) and tolerability (graded toxicity, quality of life metrics). Advanced statistical modeling and multi-criteria decision analysis would then be employed to identify the regimen(s) on the optimal trade-off frontier, providing a data-driven, patient-centric answer to the backbone selection problem beyond simple efficacy rankings.

ideas.title: Bayesian SCNA Signature Models to Predict HER2-Therapy Response Beyond IHC Thresholds
ideas.description: This research fuses Bayesian topic modeling for SCNA signatures with the clinical observation that HER2 therapy response extends beyond the IHC 3+ threshold. We propose to apply hierarchical Bayesian models, akin to latent Dirichlet allocation, to deconvolve tumors into latent 'SCNA topics' representing co-occurring genomic amplifications and deletions. The core innovation is to model the probability of clinical benefit from trastuzumab as a function of these learned SCNA signatures, rather than solely HER2 IHC score. This approach aims to discover genomically-defined subtypes within IHC 2+/FISH+ or borderline populations that harbor SCNA profiles predictive of therapeutic response, thereby providing a data-driven, probabilistic framework to refine patient selection for HER2-targeted therapies.

ideas.title: Exploiting Synthetic Lethality in MCL1-Amplified Cancers via Selective Co-Factor Disruption
ideas.description: This idea adapts a strategy from antiviral drug discovery, where allosteric inhibitors disrupt critical protein-protein interactions rather than enzymatic active sites. In HIV-1 treatment, LEDGINs inhibit integration by blocking the interaction between viral integrase and the cellular co-factor LEDGF/p75. Adapted to MCL1-amplified cancers, the research would focus on developing selective protein-protein interaction (PPI) inhibitors that disrupt the binding of amplified MCL1 to specific pro-apoptotic effector proteins (e.g., NOXA or BIM) that are essential for the cancer cell's survival balance. This approach aims to exploit a synthetic lethality: cancer cells with MCL1 amplification become uniquely dependent on these specific PPIs, so their disruption induces apoptosis with high selectivity, potentially overcoming the on-target toxicity challenges associated with direct BH3-mimetic inhibition of MCL1.

ideas.title: Toxin-Protein Conjugates for Targeted Pest Control
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from targeted pest control in agricultural biotechnology, where insect-specific carrier proteins deliver potent bacterial toxins (e.g., Bt) selectively to pests, this idea adapts that solution for cancer therapy. Instead of conjugating a standard chemotherapy drug to an anti-HER2 antibody, the proposed method involves conjugating a highly potent, engineered protein toxin (such as a modified ribonuclease or a pro-apoptotic peptide) to the antibody via a tumor-microenvironment cleavable linker. This approach aims to create a more selectively activated and powerfully cytotoxic agent specifically for HER2-positive tumor cells, mimicking the precision and potency of biological pest control agents to improve therapeutic index and reduce off-target effects in oncology.

ideas.description: This research develops and validates a predictive clinical decision-support system that integrates baseline patient multi-omic data (genomic, proteomic, metabolomic) with clinical variables to generate individualized risk scores for severe trastuzumab-anthracycline cardiotoxicity. The system uses machine learning models trained on deep-phenotyped patient cohorts to identify high-risk patients before chemotherapy initiation. This enables clinicians to proactively select safer, personalized treatment strategies, such as modified dosing sequences, alternative non-anthracycline backbones, or intensified but targeted cardioprotection, moving beyond one-size-fits-all monitoring to pre-emptive precision cardio-oncology.

ideas.title: Using High-Throughput Phenotypic Screening from Drug Discovery to Validate SCNA Gene Dependencies
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from pharmaceutical drug discovery, where high-throughput phenotypic screening identifies bioactive compounds based on a functional outcome. The analogous problem is validating the functional importance of a genomic region (an SCNA) without knowing the precise causal gene. The adapted solution is to systematically screen for compounds or perturbagens that induce synthetic lethality in cancer cell models bearing specific, unexplained SCNAs. A positive hit functionally validates the SCNA region as a dependency and points to vulnerable biological pathways. Subsequent RNAi or CRISPR screens can then be strategically focused on genes within the SCNA that are part of those implicated pathways, providing a functionally-guided validation pipeline.

ideas.title: Adapting Corrosion Inhibition Strategies for Cardiac Toxicity Monitoring and Mitigation
ideas.description: This research seed is expanded via analogy to corrosion inhibition in materials science. In that field, protective systems are monitored in real-time using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy to prevent structural failure. Adapted to oncology, the idea is to develop integrated protocols for trastuzumab-anthracycline regimens that combine continuous, non-invasive monitoring of myocardial tissue properties (e.g., via bio-impedance or speckle-tracking echocardiography) with the dynamic administration of cardioprotective agents. The solution involves creating a feedback loop where early signs of tissue stress, detected as changes in 'cardiac impedance' or deformation, trigger preemptive mitigation, allowing for safer, personalized combination therapy dosing without compromising anticancer efficacy.

ideas.title: ESSENTIAL: Experimentally Scanned Somatic InAlterability via Multi-modal Learning
ideas.description: This project systematically identifies and validates genomic regions under negative selection in cancer by distinguishing true essential constraints from technical and biological artifacts. It applies a robust statistical model, corrected for detection power across thousands of tumors from multiple sequencing platforms, to define high-confidence 'inalterable' loci. These candidates are then filtered through functional genomic annotations and directly tested for cell-essentiality using CRISPR-based perturbation in cancer models. The final output is a validated catalog of cancer-specific genomic constraints, revealing vulnerabilities distinct from developmental essential genes.

ideas.title: Context-Aware Disentanglement of Copy-Number Signals from Confounded Transcriptomic Profiles
ideas.description: This redesigned system moves beyond a direct inverse mapping to explicitly model and disentangle the confounding factors that obscure the copy-number signal in expression data. It employs a multi-modal neural framework that jointly models gene expression, inferred regulatory network activity, and tumor microenvironment context to isolate the component of expression variance attributable to somatic copy-number alterations. The model is trained on large-scale pan-cancer datasets with paired WGS and RNA-seq, using auxiliary learning tasks to force the separation of copy-number-driven expression from other influences. This results in a more robust and biologically interpretable inference of SCNA landscapes, with calibrated uncertainty estimates that reflect the inherent ambiguity of the problem.

ideas.title: Mapping Functional Targets in SCNAs via Regulatory Element Disruption
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that SCNAs act only through protein-coding genes by proposing that non-coding elements within these regions (enhancers, lncRNAs, miRNAs) are the true functional targets. Drawing an analogy from human genetics, where non-coding GWAS hits are linked to target genes via fine-mapping, we adapt this solution to cancer genomics. The adapted research idea is to systematically map chromatin interactions and regulatory landscapes in cancer models to connect non-coding elements in SCNA regions to their specific target protein-coding genes, thereby identifying the precise gene-regulator pair through which the SCNA exerts its oncogenic effect, moving beyond the assumption of simple dosage changes of the nearest or most obvious gene.

ideas.title: In vivo and co-culture CRISPR screens identify microenvironment-contingent SCNA gene dependencies
ideas.description: This project systematically tests genes within unexplained SCNA regions for essentiality under different tumor microenvironmental conditions. It employs parallel in vivo CRISPR screens in immunocompetent mouse models and in vitro co-culture screens with relevant stromal and immune cell types. This dual approach identifies which candidate driver genes represent cell-autonomous 'core' dependencies versus 'contextual' vulnerabilities that only manifest with specific microenvironmental interactions. The resulting dependency maps are stratified by context, revealing more therapeutically actionable targets whose inhibition would be effective in a given tumor ecosystem.

ideas.title: Bayesian topic modeling of genomic constraint and combinatorial alteration landscapes
ideas.description: This research develops a novel Bayesian hierarchical model that adapts and extends topic modeling to analyze pan-cancer SCNA data. The model's key innovation is to jointly learn two complementary sets of latent topics: one representing combinatorial signatures of recurrent amplifications and deletions (the 'present' topics), and another representing signatures of recurrently absent, never-altered genomic regions (the 'absent' or protective topics). By analyzing these paired topic distributions across tumors, we can identify cancer subtypes defined not only by their shared patterns of genomic gain and loss, but also by the shared essential genomic constraints they cannot violate. This unified view enables the discovery of how specific alteration programs actively avoid or are bounded by non-negotiable genomic regions, revealing fundamental rules of cancer genome evolution.

ideas.title: Genomic Conservation Mapping: Identifying Essential Constraints via Recurrent Absence
ideas.description: This research adapts the 'coldspot' analysis from conservation biology and island biogeography to cancer genomics. In biogeography, systematically mapping areas where species are consistently absent ('coldspots') reveals fundamental constraints on ecosystem assembly, such as resource limitations or physical barriers. The analogous solution is applied by performing a pan-cancer, genome-wide analysis to identify 'genomic coldspots'—regions that are never subject to somatic copy number alterations (deletions or amplifications) across large cohorts. By statistically distinguishing these consistently unaltered regions from stochastic gaps in alteration data, the study aims to characterize their features (e.g., gene essentiality, chromatin state, replication origins) to uncover the essential genomic and structural constraints that cancer evolution cannot violate, providing a dual perspective to recurrent alterations for identifying novel tumor-suppressive architectures.

ideas.title: Mechanism-aware network propagation for SCNA driver prioritization
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond generic network smoothing by first classifying broad SCNAs into probable functional modes—such as haploinsufficiency, amplified oncogene, or dominant-negative—using pre-existing genomic and functional annotations. For each mode, a tailored network propagation model is constructed, incorporating edge weights and directional constraints from relevant functional datasets (e.g., synthetic lethality networks for haploinsufficiency, signaling pathway activity for amplified genes). This mechanism-aware propagation is then applied within each SCNA region to prioritize candidate drivers, significantly improving specificity by aligning the network model with the underlying biological mechanism of tumorigenesis.

ideas.title: Phylogenetically-informed functional screening of cross-species SCNAs in a unified human organoid model
ideas.description: This project first performs a comparative analysis of SCNA landscapes across diverse mammals to nominate candidate evolutionarily conserved copy-number events. To isolate true conserved oncogenic function from species-specific genomic noise, candidate alterations are then engineered into an isogenic human epithelial organoid system using CRISPR/Cas9 and orthogonal recombination techniques to create precise, species-orthologous edits. High-throughput functional screens in these organoids assess oncogenic phenotypes like proliferation, invasion, and therapy resistance, directly testing the conserved driver potential of each alteration. This approach decouples the SCNA event from its native species' genomic context, revealing core, cross-species cancer genes and pathways with high confidence for therapeutic targeting.

ideas.title: Phylogenetic Barcoding for Single-Cell SCNA Lineage Reconstruction
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from evolutionary biology to resolve intratumor SCNA heterogeneity. In phylogenetics, the evolutionary history of species is reconstructed by comparing genetic barcodes to build phylogenetic trees showing divergence from a common ancestor. By treating the somatic copy-number alteration (SCNA) profile of individual tumor cells as analogous to these genetic barcodes, we can apply phylogenetic tree-building algorithms (e.g., maximum parsimony) to single-cell SCNA data. This computational approach will infer the most likely evolutionary lineage and branching order of tumor subclones, thereby mapping the clonal architecture and evolutionary history of the cancer directly from single-cell copy-number profiling data.

ideas.title: Multi-Gene Functional Modules in SCNAs: An Ecological Guild Analogy
ideas.description: This idea challenges the one-driver-per-region assumption in SCNA analysis by drawing an analogy from community ecology. In ecology, a 'guild' of species collectively performs a function, with the ecosystem property emerging from the group rather than a single keystone species. The analogous solution is to identify these functional groups through patterns of co-occurrence and measure their collective impact. Adapted to cancer genomics, this means treating focal SCNAs as potentially harboring a 'functional guild' of co-altered genes. The research idea is to develop methods to identify multi-gene functional modules (e.g., pathway, complex, or network cohorts) within SCNA regions using patterns of co-amplification/deletion and functional genomic data. The selective advantage of the SCNA is then modeled and tested as the combined, non-additive effect of perturbing this entire module, moving beyond single-gene driver attribution.

ideas.title: Applying Neutral Theory from Ecology to Distinguish Selected SCNAs
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from community ecology, specifically the Unified Neutral Theory, to the problem of distinguishing selected from passenger SCNAs. In ecology, neutral theory serves as a powerful null model to identify deviations indicative of selection (niche processes). We adapt this by developing a population genetics-informed neutral null model for the somatic evolution of tumors, which predicts the expected frequencies and distributions of SCNAs under the assumption of no fitness effects. SCNAs that significantly deviate from this neutral expectation—for example, by being too frequent, too recurrent in specific genomic regions, or showing non-random patterns of co-occurrence across a cohort of tumors—would be flagged as candidates for positive selection, providing a robust, model-based alternative to simple recurrence thresholds.

ideas.title: Bayesian Non-negative Matrix Factorization for SCNA Signatures with Spatial Priors
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to the analogous problem of hyperspectral image unmixing in remote sensing to the discovery of somatic copy number alteration (SCNA) signatures. In hyperspectral unmixing, Bayesian Non-negative Matrix Factorization (NMF) with spatial smoothness priors is used to decompose mixed pixels into pure material spectra and their abundances. We adapt this by modeling tumor SCNA profiles (the data matrix) as a linear combination of latent SCNA signatures (the basis matrix) and their exposures (the coefficient matrix). The key adaptation is the design of a prior for the signature basis that encodes genomic spatial structure, penalizing solutions where copy number states fluctuate unrealistically across adjacent genomic bins. This enforces the biological reality that SCNAs are large, contiguous events, leading to more interpretable and precise signatures that define cancer subtypes based on their combinatorial patterns of genomic instability.

ideas.title: A Seismological Model for Non-Uniform Somatic Copy Number Alteration Background Rates
ideas.description: This idea challenges the uniform background model for somatic copy number alterations (SCNAs) by drawing an analogy from seismology, where uniform earthquake risk models were superseded by probabilistic hazard maps. The analogous solution integrated geological variables (fault lines, tectonic stress) to predict non-uniform spatial risk. Adapted to genomics, the research would integrate molecular features known to influence DNA damage and repair—such as replication timing profiles, chromatin state (e.g., open/closed, histone marks), and nuclear architecture (e.g., lamina contact)—to construct a quantitative, genome-wide 'instability hazard map.' This map would define a variable background rate for SCNAs, providing a more accurate null model for identifying regions under positive selection in cancer genomes and for understanding the intrinsic structural vulnerability of the genome.

ideas.title: Direct measurement of locus-specific SCNA formation rates in normal somatic lineages decoupled from selection
ideas.description: This research directly tests the uniformity of the SCNA background rate by measuring the *de novo* formation rate of passenger SCNAs across the genome in normal human somatic tissues. The approach uses multi-region, deep whole-genome sequencing of clonal lineages in tissues like colon or skin from donors across a wide age range to reconstruct somatic phylogenies. By analyzing the pattern of neutral, passenger SCNAs accumulated in these lineages over time, the system can calculate the intrinsic probability of SCNA formation per cell division at specific genomic loci, independent of subsequent positive or negative selection. This direct measurement will definitively establish whether replication timing and chromatin state create a non-uniform background susceptibility or if observed SCNA landscapes are primarily shaped by selection on a uniform background.

ideas.title: Network Signal Refinement for SCNAs using Ecological Niche Modeling Principles
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from Ecology to the problem of prioritizing driver genes within broad somatic copy number alteration (SCNA) regions. In ecology, Species Distribution Models (SDMs) integrate sparse species sighting data with continuous environmental layers to predict a species' full habitat range. Analogously, we treat high-confidence driver gene annotations (e.g., from COSMIC) as sparse sightings. The continuous 'environment' is defined by each gene's multi-scale network properties within a protein-protein interaction network (e.g., functional module membership, centrality, connection patterns to known drivers). A machine learning model learns the network 'niche' of known drivers. This model then scores all genes within SCNA regions, smoothing the sparse signal across the network to prioritize candidate drivers that occupy a network context functionally analogous to established cancer genes, thereby refining the large genomic regions identified by SCNAs.

ideas.title: Distributed, Hierarchical SCNA Analysis via Federated Learning Architecture
ideas.description: Scale SCNA analysis from 3,131 to over 100,000 specimens to detect rare recurrent alterations with statistical power by adapting a solution from astrophysics. Large-scale sky surveys solve the problem of analyzing petabytes of data from globally distributed telescopes using a federated, hierarchical model: local data reduction creates standardized catalogs, enabling powerful central analysis without moving raw data. Applying this analogy, we propose a federated genomics architecture where participating institutions process tumor sequencing data locally using a standardized, validated pipeline to generate compact 'SCNA catalogs.' A central analysis hub then performs pan-cancer scans for rare recurrent alterations by statistically querying this distributed network of catalogs, dramatically reducing the computational, storage, and data-sharing burdens that would otherwise make a 100,000-tumor analysis infeasible.

ideas.title: Gene-Aware Dosage Selection Models Deciphering SCNA Fitness Landscapes
ideas.description: This framework moves beyond treating SCNAs as atomic units by explicitly modeling their internal genetic composition and the mechanistic biases of neutral somatic structural variation. It employs a generative model of background SCNA formation based on genomic fragility to create a context-specific null. Positive selection is inferred by comparing the observed dosage-alteration of genes within an SCNA—weighted by their predicted haploinsufficiency or triplosensitivity—against the null expectation, identifying SCNAs where the aggregate fitness effect of the altered gene dosages is incompatible with neutral passenger accumulation. This allows for the detection of selection on complex, heterogeneous SCNAs and reduces false positives from passenger alterations that merely overlap important genomic regions.

ideas.title: Inferring SCNAs from Expression via Seismic Tomography-Inspired Deconvolution
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to the inverse problem in seismic tomography to infer somatic copy-number alteration (SCNA) landscapes from gene expression data alone. In seismology, subsurface structure is inferred from surface wave measurements by inverting a physical propagation model. Here, we analogously model gene expression data as the observable output 'surface signal' generated from a hidden genomic 'subsurface' of SCNAs. The adapted solution involves constructing a forward model where expression is a function of copy number dosage convolved with a genomic-position-aware kernel that accounts for gene-specific regulatory effects and noise. By applying regularized inversion techniques (e.g., constrained least squares, Bayesian inference) to a large cohort of tumor expression profiles, we can computationally deconvolve the expression data to reconstruct the most likely SCNA profile for each sample, providing a cost-effective and widely applicable alternative to direct genomic measurement.

ideas.title: Systematic functional dissection of SCNAs to isolate causal contributions of coding and non-coding elements
ideas.description: This project directly tests whether non-coding elements within SCNAs are functional drivers by experimentally isolating their effects from those of co-amplified or co-deleted protein-coding genes. Using precise genome engineering, we create matched cellular models where either the protein-coding genes or the candidate non-coding elements within a defined SCNA are specifically inactivated. By comparing the phenotypic consequences of these orthogonal perturbations in high-throughput assays, we can assign causal functional impact to specific genomic element classes, moving beyond correlative association to definitive mechanistic insight.
